dossiers.name: NetMHCpan-4.1 (EL score / %Rank_EL)
dossiers.admits_evidence: The predictor scores any user-supplied peptide (or FASTA it digests) of the 20 standard amino acids, 8-14mers; it is proteome-agnostic and does not require the peptide to be annotated in any reference proteome. Service page: peptide-list or FASTA input, lengths 8-14 (default 8-11) — https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/NetMHCpan-4.1/ . Reynisson et al. NAR 2020, W449: "%Rank ... comparing to a distribution of scores from random natural peptides", per-peptide scoring independent of source annotation — https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/48/W1/W449/5837056 . Caveat: only standard amino acids; no non-standard/modified residues. Our IR/poison-exon/frameshift 9-11mers are standard-AA arbitrary strings, so they are admitted.
dossiers.availability: web-server-only for click use; downloadable standalone (Linux/Darwin) under DTU academic license (free to publicly-funded academic/research institutions; commercial/non-academic must license via health-software@dtu.dk). Not open-source, not redistributable.
dossiers.inputs: Required: (1) peptide as a plain sequence list OR a FASTA that the tool digests into k-mers; standard 20 AA only; lengths 8-14 (default 8-11) — our 9-11mers are in range. (2) MHC allele in HLA nomenclature, e.g. HLA-A*02:01 (also accepts custom full-length MHC protein sequence via FASTA). No expression, no flanking-context, no cleavage inputs required for the EL score (the EL model already folds in antigen-processing signal learned from MS eluted-ligand data). Runs offline as a standalone binary on the A*02:01 registry directly.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two paired EL outputs. Score_EL = model likelihood that the peptide is a naturally presented MHC ligand (0-1-like, HIGHER = more likely presented / better). %Rank_EL = that score's percentile against random natural peptides for the same allele (LOWER = better; allele-normalized so it is comparable across alleles). Canonical thresholds: Strong Binder %Rank_EL < 0.5%, Weak Binder < 2% (user-adjustable). Also emits %Rank_BA, Score_BA, and affinity nM for reference, but %Rank_EL is the field-default presentation ranker. For a ranking harness, sort ascending by %Rank_EL (or descending by Score_EL).
dossiers.validation_summary: Reynisson et al. NAR 2020: trained via NNAlign_MA on ~13.2M data points spanning binding-affinity + MS eluted-ligand data across 250 MHC-I molecules with concurrent motif deconvolution of multi-allelic EL data. Validated on independent, training-disjoint benchmarks: MS eluted-ligand sets scored by AUC/PPV, and CD8 T-cell epitope datasets scored by FRANK (epitope rank among decoys). NetMHCpan-4.1 outperformed MHCflurry, MixMHCpred, and NetMHCpan-4.0 on most benchmarks. Held-out design used allele/data partitions absent from training. https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/48/W1/W449/5837056 (open: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7319546/)
dossiers.integration_notes: Strong drop-in baseline for #736's A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment benchmark. It is the same conceptual quantity as our pipeline's presentation ranker (MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor) and an even closer conceptual sibling to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds's presentation term, so it is the reference the field expects to see. Wiring: run the standalone binary per registry peptide with -a HLA-A02:01 -p (peptide-list mode); parse %Rank_EL (or Score_EL). Direction alignment: our log-odds is HIGHER = more immunogenic, and Score_EL is HIGHER = better while %Rank_EL is LOWER = better — negate %Rank_EL (or use Score_EL directly) before computing a shared rank/AUC so both rankers point the same way. Because n=1 hard true-negative, report enrichment/rank-based metrics (FRANK-style, top-k recall, ROC-AUC of positives-vs-decoys) not calibrated specificity. NOTE: NetMHCpan is presentation-only and TCR-blind, so it is a floor baseline; any lift of calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds over it is the interesting signal. License: standalone academic download is fine for internal benchmarking; results are publishable, but the binary itself is not redistributable, so do not vendor it into the open-benchmark repo — document the version + command instead. Beware EL data leakage: some registry positives may overlap NetMHCpan-4.1 EL training ligands, which would inflate its apparent ranking; flag/annotate any known-overlap peptides.

_verdict.corrections:
No corrections needed to the load-bearing claims. Minor precision note: I directly verified only the Linux standalone package page (https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/cgi-bin/sw_request?software=netMHCpan&version=4.1&packageversion=4.1b&platform=Linux). The dossier says "Linux/Darwin"; a Darwin build for v4.1 is plausible (DTU ships "packages for the most common UNIX platforms" and a Darwin page exists for v4.2) but the exact 4.1/Darwin package is unverified.</corrections>
<parameter name="residual_risk">(1) macOS/Darwin standalone for v4.1 specifically is unverified - confirm the Darwin package exists before promising a native macOS run; Linux is confirmed. (2) EL training data leakage: some A*02:01 registry positives may overlap NetMHCpan-4.1's MS eluted-ligand training ligands, inflating its apparent ranking - annotate known-overlap peptides. (3) Non-redistributable license (non-transferable, single-Site, modification prohibited, no commercialization) - do NOT vendor the binary into the open-benchmark repo; pin the version + exact command instead. (4) Standard 20 AA only; the registry is standard-AA so fine, but any peptide with non-standard/modified residues would need handling (X wildcard is tolerated per the paper).

dossiers.name: NetMHCpan-4.2 (EL score)
dossiers.admits_evidence: NetMHCpan is a purely sequence-based per-peptide/allele predictor: it scores any submitted 8-14mer of standard amino acids against a chosen allele with no reference-proteome membership check, and DTU ships a dedicated "neoepitope prediction" mode. Custom/arbitrary peptide lists and FASTA are accepted inputs (service page: "Peptide sequences (FASTA... peptide list...)"; paper describes a neoepitope-prediction application on CEDAR cancer neoepitopes). CAVEAT (not a hard lock, an inductive-bias caveat): the network was trained/fine-tuned exclusively on naturally eluted ligands + IEDB/CEDAR epitopes and "peptides with post-translational modifications" and non-standard residues are excluded; nothing forbids frameshift/junction-derived sequences of canonical AAs, but the presentation prior is learned from canonical natural ligands. Service page: https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/NetMHCpan-4.2/ ; paper: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12367478/
dossiers.availability: web-server + academic-download standalone (license-gated for non-academic). Standalone stand-alone package for common UNIX platforms; academic users download via DTU request form, others must contact health-software@dtu.dk (no open-source repo, not FOSS). Unverified: whether a 4.2 standalone tarball is already published vs web-only at time of writing.
dossiers.inputs: Per peptide: peptide sequence (standard 20 AAs only; 8-14mers, our 9-11mers fully covered) + one MHC-I allele (e.g. HLA-A*02:01). Input as peptide list, FASTA (with length selection), or peptide-MHC pairs; up to 20 alleles/submission on the server. No expression, flanking, or cleavage-context input required for the EL %Rank (the EL model already bundles a processing/presentation signal analogous to MHCflurry's presentation predictor). Custom MHC via full-length protein sequence upload also supported.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Primary presentation output is the Eluted-Ligand (EL) likelihood plus its EL %Rank (percentile vs a random natural-peptide background for that allele). DIRECTION: EL raw score HIGHER = stronger predicted presentation; EL %Rank LOWER = better (top presenter), with the paper's non-binder cut at %Rank > ~20 (conventional strong/weak thresholds ~0.5/2). Optional BA (affinity) output is secondary. For a ranking harness use EL %Rank ascending (or 1 - EL raw / -EL%Rank) so direction matches "higher = better presenter." Note our pipeline's calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds is higher=more immunogenic, so invert %Rank when co-plotting.
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained/evaluated on 197,504 BA measurements + 792,905 EL positives across 465 immunopeptidomics samples (201 MHC-I molecules); transfer-learning fine-tune on 42,921 IEDB pathogen epitope points (10,302 pos) and 5,172 CEDAR cancer-neoepitope points (1,188 pos). Held-out design: cross-validation and external test sets built to guarantee NO 8-mer overlap between train and test partitions/epitopes. Metrics: AUC, AUC0.1 (ROC to 10% FPR), and PPV at rank. New in 4.2 vs 4.1: deletion/bulge structural features for >9mers (BLOSUM50 composition of deleted residues + positional MHC-interaction frequency from PDB) and transfer learning; authors report cancer<->pathogen epitope transferability is limited (gains only when fine-tune source matches test source). Whether these changes alter non-canonical behavior vs 4.1: UNVERIFIED. Paper: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12367478/
dossiers.integration_notes: Strong drop-in baseline for the A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment harness. Run each registry 9-11mer through NetMHCpan-4.2 with allele HLA-A*02:01, take EL %Rank, rank ascending, and compute the same enrichment/ranking metric (e.g. AUC/positive-rank) you apply to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds. Both are presentation-family scores, so this is an apples-to-apples MHCflurry-vs-NetMHCpan presentation comparison; it is a presentation baseline, NOT an immunogenicity baseline, so frame it as "does presentation %Rank alone recover the functional positives" vs our calibrated log-odds. Sign handling: invert %Rank (use -%Rank or 1-percentile/100) so higher=better before merging into the ranking table. For reproducibility prefer the academic standalone binary (SNU is publicly-funded academic → eligible) over the web form so the benchmark is scriptable and versioned; pin the exact 4.2 package version. Caveat to state in #736: EL prior is trained on canonical natural ligands, so systematically lower EL scores for junction/frameshift peptides could reflect training bias rather than true non-presentation - a documented limitation, not a disqualifier. All capability claims cite the DTU service page and PMC12367478; standalone-tarball availability for 4.2 specifically is unverified.

dossiers.name: MHCflurry 2.0 (presentation) — Class1PresentationPredictor
dossiers.admits_evidence: MHCflurry is purely sequence-based: it accepts any peptide string of standard amino acids with no proteome lookup or annotation requirement. The CLI/API take peptides directly (`mhcflurry-predict --alleles HLA-A0201 --peptides SIINFEKL`), and Class1PresentationPredictor.predict() takes a `peptides` list plus optional free-text `n_flanks`/`c_flanks` you supply yourself, so intron-retention / poison-exon / novel-junction / frameshift 8-15mers are admitted identically to canonical peptides (flanks are just the junction-spanning transcript context you compute). Docs: https://openvax.github.io/mhcflurry/commandline_tutorial.html and https://openvax.github.io/mhcflurry/api.html (Class1PresentationPredictor.predict signature: peptides, alleles, sample_names, n_flanks, c_flanks, ...). Repo: https://github.com/openvax/mhcflurry . CAVEAT: the antigen-processing sub-model was trained on natural MS ligands, so its cleavage motif priors are learned from canonical processing; it still scores non-canonical peptides but its calibration on frameshift/junction context is unvalidated (unverified for splice specifically).
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python; github.com/openvax/mhcflurry; pip install mhcflurry + mhcflurry-downloads fetch)
dossiers.inputs: Required: peptide sequence (8-15mers; MS-presentation models best-supported over ~8-11mers) + one or more MHC-I alleles (pan-allele; supports 14,993 class I alleles incl. HLA-A*02:01; pass ≤6 patient alleles as a genotype list, not repeated). Optional but recommended: N-flank and C-flank upstream/downstream source-protein sequences ("when available you should provide the upstream (N-flank) and downstream (C-flank) sequences from the source proteins ... for a small boost in accuracy" — https://openvax.github.io/mhcflurry/python_tutorial.html). No expression input. No proteome annotation required.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Primary: `presentation_score` in [0,1], HIGHER = more likely presented (docs example: SIINFEKL 0.0125 vs NLVPMVATV 0.9755 — https://openvax.github.io/mhcflurry/python_tutorial.html). Also emits `presentation_percentile` where LOWER = stronger presenter (percentile rank of the presentation_score against a fixed reference set of random peptides for the allele, same convention as affinity_percentile / NetMHCpan %rank). Intermediate columns returned: `affinity` (predicted IC50 nM, LOWER=stronger), `affinity_percentile` (lower=better), `processing_score` (0-1, higher=more cleavable), `best_allele`, plus peptide/n_flank/c_flank/sample_name. Our pipeline's `presentation_class` (strong/weak/non) is thresholded off the percentile. It is a PRESENTATION likelihood, NOT a T-cell immunogenicity probability.
dossiers.validation_summary: O'Donnell et al., Cell Systems 2020 (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405471220302398 ; https://www.cell.com/cell-systems/fulltext/S2405-4712(20)30331-8). The integrated presentation predictor combines a new pan-allele binding-affinity model (variable-length up to 15-mers, 14,993 alleles, single NN ensemble) with an antigen-processing model trained to discriminate published mass-spec MHC-I ligands from unobserved decoy peptides (restricted to strong predicted binders). Held-out design: evaluated on held-out multi-allele mass-spec experiments; the integrated model outperformed each component alone and beat NetMHCpan 4.0 and MixMHCpred 2.0.2. The processing model favored established antigen-processing motifs and showed quantitative agreement with an INDEPENDENT proteasome-cleaved-peptide dataset. KEY GAP for #736: all validation is against MS-observed PRESENTATION, never measured T-cell immunogenicity — so as a #736 baseline it is a presentation prior, not an immunogenicity predictor.
dossiers.integration_notes: Zero new dependency — this is exactly the predictor the pipeline already runs (Class1PresentationPredictor, post-MHCflurry stage), so the A*02:01 ranking/enrichment harness can pull `presentation_score` (or `-presentation_percentile`) straight from the existing `mhc_presentation.tsv` columns with no re-inference. Wire it in as the PRESENTATION-FLOOR baseline sitting next to `calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds`: rank candidates by presentation_score DESCENDING (equivalently presentation_percentile ASCENDING) and compute the same enrichment/AUC-of-ranking metric #736 uses for the calibrated score. It is the correct null-to-beat: if calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds does not out-enrich raw presentation_score on the 81-positive A*02:01 registry, the calibration adds nothing. Handling notes: (1) direction differs between the two percentile/score columns — normalize both to "higher=better" before ranking (negate percentile); (2) restrict the harness to A*02:01 rows (89% of registry) to match the underpowered no-scalar posture; (3) supply n_flank/c_flank from the junction transcript context for a fair processing signal, since omitting them silently degrades processing_score; (4) because presentation ≠ immunogenicity, report it explicitly as a presentation baseline, not a competitor immunogenicity model. Fixed inputs (deterministic models, no expression term) make it reproducible for a frozen benchmark.

dossiers.name: MixMHCpred 2.2 / 3.0
dossiers.admits_evidence: MixMHCpred is a pure sequence->score function: a per-length position-specific scoring matrix / motif model (v2.2) or pan-allele NN motif predictor (v3.0) that scores ANY input peptide of length 8-14 against the allele motif; it does not consult a proteome index or restrict to annotated peptides. The README states it accepts arbitrary peptide lists (FASTA or plain text), and the %Rank is calibrated against ~700k random-proteome peptides purely as a background, not as a membership filter. So intron-retention / poison-exon / novel-junction / frameshift 9-11mers score identically to canonical ones as long as they are standard amino acids in the 8-14 length window. Caveat: length must be 8-14 (our 9-11mers qualify) and only the 20 standard residues are supported. GitHub README: https://github.com/GfellerLab/MixMHCpred ; Genome Medicine 2025 (Tadros et al.): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11927126/
dossiers.availability: open-source (Bash/Python/C++ wrapper, GitHub GfellerLab/MixMHCpred; v3.0 released 2025-09-24). Free for academic use; for-profit use requires a separate license from the Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research (nbulgin@lcr.org). Also exposed via the Gfeller lab web server. v3.0 requires MAFFT for the pan-allele alignment step.
dossiers.inputs: Required: (1) peptide list, 8-14mers, FASTA or plain-text one-per-line; (2) MHC-I allele list, comma-separated HLA nomenclature without HLA- prefix/colons (e.g. A0201,B0702). NO expression values, NO flanking sequence, NO cleavage/processing context, NO patient genotype file needed. For our harness: pass the 9-11mer splice peptide and allele A0201. v3.0 additionally needs MAFFT installed (pan-allele alignment); v2.2 is allele-table-driven (allele must be in its supported per-allele set).
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two numbers per peptide-allele: (1) raw Score (log-average of position-specific motif weights, length- and SD-normalized) - HIGHER = more likely a ligand; (2) %Rank - percentile of that raw score against ~700k random human-proteome 8-14mers, LOWER = better (0=best, 100=worst); typical presenter cutoffs %Rank < 2 (strong) / < 10 (weak). It also reports the best-assigned allele when multiple are supplied. IMPORTANT semantics caveat for #736: this is a PRESENTATION / EL-ligand-likelihood score (built purely from immunopeptidomics eluted-ligand data), NOT an immunogenicity score - it sits at the same layer as MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor, upstream of our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds, not parallel to it.
dossiers.validation_summary: Authors validated v3.0 (Tadros/Gfeller et al., Genome Medicine 2025) by leave-one-allele-out cross-validation over 143 MHC-I alleles (127 human HLA + mouse/other species) / 511,553 eluted ligands, plus external benchmarks on independent samples (meningioma, immunopeptidomics, COVID-19 cohorts) using AUC (4-fold and 99-fold negative excess), precision-recall AUC for top-rank enrichment, and motif/length-distribution Euclidean distance. Reported similar performance to NetMHCpan and MHCflurry and improvement over BigMHC on held-out alleles. v2.2 (Gfeller et al. 2023, ResearchGate 366891709) paired with PRIME2.0 for TCR-recognition/immunogenicity and validated on SARS-CoV-2 CD8+ epitopes. Validation is on naturally-presented ligands; non-canonical/frameshift/splice peptides are not specifically benchmarked.
dossiers.integration_notes: Wire it as a PRESENTATION-layer baseline ranker on the A*02:01 (allele string "A0201") enrichment harness, sitting alongside MHCflurry rather than alongside calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds. Feed each registry 9-11mer + A0201; collect both raw Score and %Rank. For ranking/enrichment use %Rank ascending (or -Score) so direction matches "better epitope = smaller rank." Because 89% of the registry is HLA-A*02:01 - the single best-characterized allele - MixMHCpred is at its strongest here, making it a clean apples-to-apples EL baseline. Two things to control for: (a) it is presentation, not immunogenicity, so frame it as the "presentation-only null model" the immunogenicity log-odds should beat/complement, not as a competing immunogenicity predictor; (b) v3.0's MAFFT dependency and pan-allele path add a runtime step - for an A0201-only harness v2.2's per-allele table is lighter and A0201 is natively supported. Standard-AA/length-8-14 only: filter any registry peptide outside 8-14mer or with non-standard residues before scoring. License is fine for academic benchmarking; note the for-profit clause if the harness is ever redistributed.

dossiers.name: HLAthena (Sarkizova et al., Nat Biotechnol 2020)
dossiers.admits_evidence: HLAthena is a sequence-based presentation predictor: the web app's minimal input is "a peptide column containing the peptide sequence, and if only this column is provided, only the MSintrinsic (MSi) predictors will be run" — no requirement that the peptide come from the annotated proteome, so any 8-11mer (intron-retention/poison-exon/novel-junction/frameshift) can be scored by MSi. Caveat: the context model MSiC needs "ctex_up and ctex_dn columns ... the 30 upstream and 30 downstream amino acid residues for each peptide", which for a non-canonical peptide must be taken from the NOVEL translated ORF (our splice contig), not the reference proteome; MSiCE additionally needs expression as TPM. Source: HLAthena Google Group help thread https://groups.google.com/g/hlathena and http://hlathena.tools/. Percentile ranks are benchmarked against a random-proteome (UniProt/SWISS-PROT) background, but only the background is proteomic — the query peptide is arbitrary.
dossiers.availability: web-server-only (primary): free "for research purposes only" at http://hlathena.tools; a dockerized image and a Terra/FireCloud workflow are referenced for large/batch runs (unverified whether the container is openly pullable without request). Commercial use gated: "For commercial usage inquiries please contact the authors or the Broad Institute." No canonical open-source GitHub repo of the trained models located (unverified).
dossiers.inputs: Required: peptide sequence (8-11mer; MSi runs on this alone). Optional for richer models: per-peptide flanking context ctex_up (30 aa upstream) + ctex_dn (30 aa downstream) to enable MSiC; gene/transcript expression as TPM (or log(TPM+1)) to enable MSiCE. Allele is selected per run (models are allele-and-length-specific plus a pan-allele/pan-length model). For A*02:01 that allele is directly covered.
dossiers.integration_notes: Wire it as a second per-peptide presentation column on the same A*02:01 candidate rows we already feed to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds. Feed the raw peptide (MSi) for a like-for-like presentation baseline; because our contig translation already yields the novel ORF, we CAN populate ctex_up/ctex_dn from the 30 aa flanking the epitope within that novel ORF to run MSiC (do NOT pull flanks from the reference proteome for frameshift/novel-junction peptides), and add expression TPM for MSiCE when available. Practical blocker for a reproducible open harness: no verified openly-downloadable standalone/model artifact — scoring may require the web app (manual/batched) or the docker/Terra path, so treat HLAthena as a reference/secondary baseline unless the container is confirmed pullable. Rank direction differs from a %rank tool (higher raw MSi score = better), so normalize to a rank before comparing enrichment against our log-odds. Same presentation-vs-affinity framing as our MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor, so it is a conceptually matched presentation baseline, not an orthogonal immunogenicity signal.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Presentation-likelihood score per peptide-allele (probability-like, 0-1), HIGHER = more likely presented. Verified MSiC banding from the tool help: ">0.95 strong binding, >0.90 normal binding, >0.80 weak binding". Three nested variants: MSi (intrinsic sequence/binding motif), MSiC (adds flanking-context/cleavage), MSiCE (adds expression). A percentile rank is also emitted (rank of the peptide's score against a random-proteome background); by the standard convention LOWER percentile = stronger/more-likely-presented, with an assignment cutoff around ≤0.5 (exact HLAthena percentile bands unverified). It is NOT an IC50 affinity and NOT a calibrated immunogenicity/T-cell-recognition score.
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on mono-allelic immunopeptidomics: >185,000 peptides eluted from 95 mono-allelic HLA-A/-B/-C/-G cell lines (prompt's "~8.5M peptides / 92 alleles" is unverified against the paper; verified figures are >185k peptides / 95 alleles). Held-out validation reported ~1.5-fold improvement in positive predictive value over existing tools (NetMHCpan/MHCflurry class). On tumor immunopeptidomes it recovered >75% of experimentally observed HLA-bound peptides across 11 patient-derived tumor cell lines. Validation is presentation/elution recovery (immunopeptidome), NOT measured T-cell immunogenicity — so it validates the presentation step our benchmark sits downstream of, not the immunogenicity endpoint of #736.

dossiers.name: BigMHC (BigMHC_EL / BigMHC_IM)
dossiers.admits_evidence: The model consumes only (a) the raw peptide sequence and (b) a 30-residue MHC pseudosequence derived from the allele name — no proteome annotation, gene ID, or reading-frame context is required. Input is a CSV with a peptide column + optional allele column; there is no proteome lookup step. Because scoring depends solely on peptide+pseudosequence, any arbitrary sequence of length >=8 (incl. IR / poison-exon / novel-junction / frameshift peptides) is scored. Verified: GitHub README input spec (https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc) and the architecture description "each Wide BiLSTM cell unroll consumes the entire MHC representation while recurrently processing the variable-length epitope ... minimum epitope length of 8" (https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690.full.pdf ; paper https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6). Caveat: max-length not explicitly stated in README; trained on MHC-I ligands (canonically 8-15mers), so 9-11mers are squarely in-range. No length cap that would exclude our 9-11mers is documented (unverified above ~15mer).
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python 3.9 / PyTorch 1.13; LSTM ensemble; OS-agnostic, CPU-only OK, no GPU required)
dossiers.inputs: CSV: one peptide per row (min length 8; MHC-I range). MHC-I allele either as a per-row column or a single -a=HLA-A*02:01 flag applied to all rows; allele name is fuzzy-matched across schemes (HLA-A*02:01 / A*02:01 / HLAA0201). NO flanking N/C-terminal context required, NO expression input, NO proteome annotation. Column indices are zero-based (-p peptide col, -a/-c allele col, header skip via -c). Example: python predict.py -i=input.csv -m=im -a=HLA-A*02:01 -d="cpu". Verified: README (https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc).
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Per-(peptide,allele) scalar in [0,1], appended as a new column; HIGHER = more presented (BigMHC_EL, eluted-ligand presentation probability) or more immunogenic (BigMHC_IM, transfer-learned neoepitope immunogenicity probability). Two separate heads selected by -m=el vs -m=im. It is a RAW model probability, NOT a %rank/percentile (unlike NetMHCpan/MHCflurry, BigMHC emits no built-in rank normalization) — so for cross-peptide comparison you rank on the raw score directly. Direction matches our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds (higher = more immunogenic). Exact output column name not documented in README (unverified — confirm by running predict.py on a fixture). Sources: README + paper https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6.
dossiers.validation_summary: EL head: pan-allelic ensemble of 7 deep nets trained on MS eluted-ligand pMHC data; on a held-out test of 45,409 MHC ligands among 900,592 random negatives it reached AUROC=0.9733, AUPRC=0.8779, beating 4 SOTA presentation classifiers. IM head: transfer-learned on antigen-specific immune-response (T-cell) assay data; authors report significantly HIGHER PRECISION (PPV) than 7 SOTA immunogenicity models at identifying immunogenic neoepitopes — i.e. validated as a positive-enrichment/top-ranked-precision task, the same framing #736 needs, not a calibrated-probability task. Held-out design is peptide-level test splits; immunogenicity cohorts are curated T-cell-response datasets (TESLA-style assay-validated positives). Sources: paper abstract https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6 ; preprint https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690v3.full . Caveat (unverified): none of the reported validation is splice-junction-derived — all canonical point-mutation neoepitopes, so #736 would be an out-of-distribution stress test for it too.
dossiers.integration_notes: Wire BigMHC_IM as the primary external comparator sitting directly beside calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds in the A*02:01 ranking/enrichment harness (same direction: higher=more immunogenic; rank-based enrichment/AUROC/top-k so the raw-probability-vs-log-odds scale mismatch is irrelevant). Also run BigMHC_EL as a presentation comparator next to MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor. Concretely: emit one CSV of our A*02:01 registry peptides, call `python predict.py -m=im -a=HLA-A*02:01 -d=cpu` (CPU-only, no GPU, fits the $0 local core) and `-m=el` likewise; join the appended score column back on peptide key. Pin the repo commit + record the 7-model ensemble weights (~5GB repo) for reproducibility. Two integration cautions: (1) confirm the output column name and that no silent length filter drops any 9-11mer by running on a 3-row fixture first; (2) LICENSE is a CUSTOM NON-COMMERCIAL academic license (Karchin Lab/JHU), NOT OSI open-source — fine to run + cite in an academic open benchmark, but we must NOT redistribute BigMHC weights inside our repo/benchmark artifact and should document "install from upstream" + note the non-commercial restriction in the #736 benchmark README. Verified license: https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc (LICENSE).

dossiers.name: PRIME 2.0 (PRedictor of class I IMmunogenic Epitopes) — Gfeller Lab
dossiers.admits_evidence: The tool accepts any 8-14 aa peptide supplied as a plain text/FASTA list; there is no proteome-annotation/lookup step - peptide string + HLA-I allele are the only per-peptide inputs, so a splice-/intron-retention-/frameshift-derived 9-11mer is scored like any other sequence. CAVEAT 1: %Rank is normalized against "random peptides from the human proteome (length 8 to 14)" - the reference distribution is the canonical proteome, not junction/frameshift space. CAVEAT 2: training + validation used experimentally verified NEO-EPITOPES (point-mutation neoantigens) + IEDB negatives; splice/frameshift antigens are outside the validated domain. Sources: https://github.com/GfellerLab/PRIME (input format + %Rank definition); paper PMC9811684 https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9811684/ (training/validation on neo-epitopes).
dossiers.availability: open-source (bash wrapper + compiled binaries, academic-use-only license); repo GfellerLab/PRIME. NOTE: live repo now ships PRIME 2.1 (paper describes 2.0). For-profit use requires a separate license from the Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research (contact nbulgin@lcr.org). Citation required: Gfeller et al., Cell Systems 2023.
dossiers.inputs: Required per peptide: (1) peptide sequence, 8-14 aa (9-mers modal; our 9-11mers all in range); (2) one or more HLA-I alleles (A0201 / A2501 / B0801 naming). Input = text or FASTA file. NO gene-expression, flanking-sequence, or abundance input. Runtime dependency: MixMHCpred (README says v3.0+; paper pairs PRIME2.0 with MixMHCpred2.2), installed and passed via -mix; PRIME calls it internally for the presentation feature.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two numbers per peptide/allele: a raw PRIME "score" and a "%Rank". Operative metric = %Rank = fraction of random human-proteome peptides (len 8-14) scoring >= the input. Direction: LOWER %Rank = MORE immunogenic (authors use %Rank <= 0.5 as strong-candidate cutoff). This is INVERTED vs our log-odds (higher = more immunogenic), so sign-flip / rank-ascending before correlating with calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds. Output columns: peptide, best (lowest) %Rank across alleles, PRIME score for that allele, MixMHCpred %Rank, best allele, per-allele metrics.
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on 596 experimentally verified immunogenic neo-epitopes (10 8-mers, 391 9-mers, 148 10-mers, 47 11-mers) from ~70 neo-antigen studies + IEDB, vs 6,084 verified non-immunogenic peptides + 58,905 random negatives (~99 per positive, same source protein); immunogenicity assay = IFN-gamma ELISpot / measured T-cell recognition. Validation: standard 10-fold, leave-one-ALLELE-out, and leave-one-STUDY-out CV (the latter two probe unseen-allele / unseen-batch generalization - directly relevant to a small A*02:01 registry). Benchmarked by AUC against MixMHCpred2.2, NetMHCpan4.1, MHCflurry2.0, HLAthena, PRIME1.0 - reported improved discrimination across all schemes (exact AUCs in Fig 3, not quoted - unverified numerically). Prospective proof-of-concept: predicted SARS-CoV-2 CD8+ epitopes later confirmed. Source: PMC9811684.
dossiers.integration_notes: FIT: strong drop-in baseline for the #736 A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment harness - CPU-only, no GPU/expression/structure, training domain (ELISpot-validated point-mutation neoantigens) is the closest published analogue to our functional-scorable registry, 8-14mer window covers our 9-11mers. WIRING: (1) install MixMHCpred (v2.2 to match paper, or README-pinned 3.0+), point PRIME at it via -mix. (2) score each registry peptide with allele A0201, collect %Rank. (3) DIRECTION FIX: %Rank is lower-is-better -> negate / rank ascending to align with calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds (higher-is-better) before rank-correlation / top-k recall / AUROC. (4) registry is 89% A*02:01 and %Rank is allele-specific + proteome-normalized, so holding allele = A*02:01 removes cross-allele scale confounds - clean apples-to-apples. CAVEATS for the deck: (a) normalization reference + training set are canonical proteome peptides, so junction/frameshift bias is unmeasured - report as an off-domain baseline, not a validated splice scorer. (b) The prompt's 'logistic-regression' label is PRIME 1.0; PRIME 2.0 is a NEURAL NETWORK (28 input nodes: 1 MixMHCpred2.2 -log(%rank) presentation feature + 20 TCR-contact-position aa-frequency nodes + 7 one-hot length nodes) - cite architecture correctly. (c) MixMHCpred2.2 covers ~119 HLA-I alleles; A*02:01 well-covered, no allele gap. (d) academic-use license only - fine for an open benchmark, note for-profit clause on redistribution.

dossiers.name: BigMHC (IM / immunogenicity mode)
dossiers.admits_evidence: Inference takes a plain CSV of (peptide, MHC allele) rows with no proteome lookup, no gene/transcript ID, and no length filter in predict.py: "python predict.py -i=../data/example1.csv -m=el ..."; the model uses a length-8 window and imposes only a minimum epitope length of 8 (no annotation/canonical constraint). Any 9-11mer string is scored as-is. README: https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc ; predict.py sigmoid output confirmed at https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc (src/predict.py); length/window statement from paper abstract/search: https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6 . Caveat: proteome-agnostic at INFERENCE, but IM transfer-learning data (PRIME + TESLA) are canonical point-mutation neoepitopes, so no splice/frameshift examples were in training (distribution caveat, not an input restriction).
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python/PyTorch 1.13, code + all 7 ensemble weights bundled, ~5GB clone) BUT license-gated: "BigMHC Academic License" — noncommercial/academic only, commercial use requires contacting the licensor (not an OSI/SPDX license)
dossiers.inputs: Minimal: peptide sequence + one MHC-I allele per row (single allele broadcast to all rows via -a, or a per-row allele column). No expression, no flanking sequence, no transcript/gene ID required. Allele fuzzy-matched, multiple formats accepted (HLA-A*02:01 / A*02:01 / HLAA0201 / A0201 all equivalent). Peptide length: minimum 8 (length-8 sliding window); pan-allele. A*02:01 supported. Run: `python predict.py -i=input.csv -m=im -t=<col> -d=cpu`.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Single scalar per peptide-MHC pair, sigmoid-transformed to [0,1] (torch.sigmoid in src/predict.py), written to a column named after the model (IM mode). HIGHER = more immunogenic. It is a model probability-like score (relative immunogenicity likelihood), NOT a %rank and NOT calibrated affinity/IC50 — direction is directly usable for ranking/enrichment. (README does not formally define units; treat as a monotone immunogenicity score, higher-is-better.)
dossiers.validation_summary: Ensemble of 7 pan-allele deep nets trained on MS eluted-ligand presentation (EL: test AUROC=0.9733, AUPRC=0.8779 on 45,409 ligands vs 900,592 decoys), then IM mode transfer-learned (retraining final + penultimate FC layers) on the PRIME and TESLA antigen-specific immune-response datasets. IM validated on a held-out test set ("set T"): best neoepitope immunogenicity predictor of the group (PRIME best on infectious-disease epitopes, BigMHC IM best on neoepitopes); primary metric = precision / positive predictive value, reported as significantly higher than 7 SOTA models (incl. PRIME, NetMHCpan-derived) for identifying immunogenic neoepitopes. Infectious-disease immunogenicity separately evaluated on IEDB (linear peptides, T-cell assays, MHC-I, human). Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6 ; preprint https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690v1.full
dossiers.integration_notes: Strong drop-in baseline for the #736 A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment harness. (1) Inputs are exactly what the registry has — a peptide string + a single allele — so build a CSV of the 81 functional-scorable rows (9-11mers, min-length-8 satisfied), set allele column to the per-row HLA (89% A*02:01), run `predict.py -m=im -d=cpu`. No expression/flank engineering, no proteome resolution — non-canonical splice/frameshift peptides score identically to canonical ones. (2) Score is higher=more-immunogenic in [0,1], same direction as our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds, so rank both, compute the same enrichment/AUROC-style ranking metric, and place BigMHC-IM as an external column beside our score. (3) Because n=1 hard true-negative, use it as a RANKING/enrichment comparator only, not a specificity/PPV benchmark (the authors' own headline PPV claim needs real negatives we don't have). (4) License caveat: BigMHC Academic License is noncommercial — fine for an academic open benchmark, but flag it in the #736 manifest since the harness is otherwise "open"; it is NOT redistributable for commercial use. (5) Distribution caveat to note in writeup: IM was trained on canonical PRIME/TESLA neoepitopes, so scores on splice/frameshift peptides are out-of-training-distribution — a legitimate thing for #736 to probe, not a blocker. (6) Env is isolated (PyTorch 1.13 / Python 3.9) — run in its own venv, not the pipeline's MHCflurry env.

dossiers.name: DeepImmuno-CNN
dossiers.admits_evidence: Peptide is encoded purely from sequence physicochemistry ("reduced PCA feature space of 566 well-curated amino acid physicochemical features from the AAindex1 database"), not from any proteome/annotation lookup, so it will score arbitrary/novel sequences including intron-retention, poison-exon, novel-junction and frameshift-derived peptides. CAVEAT: hard length gate to 9- and 10-mers only ("Currently, Deepimmuno-CNN only supports peptides in the length of 9 and 10"); 11-mers in our registry cannot be scored, and HLA input is limited to alleles present in its paratope dictionary. Paper: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8135853/ ; README: https://github.com/frankligy/DeepImmuno
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python, MIT License, GitHub frankligy/DeepImmuno) + public web server
dossiers.inputs: A (peptide, HLA) pair only. Peptide: a 9-mer or 10-mer amino-acid string (arbitrary sequence accepted; 11-mers rejected). HLA: single class-I allele in asterisk notation, e.g. HLA-A*0201, HLA-B*5801, HLA-C*0702 (must be in DeepImmuno's paratope/pseudo-sequence dictionary; A*02:01 supported and is the canonical example). NO expression level, NO flanking-sequence, NO proteasomal-cleavage inputs. CLI: `python3 deepimmuno-cnn.py --mode single --epitope HPPLMNVER --hla HLA-A*0201`, or `--mode multiple --intdir <csv of peptide,HLA> --outdir <dir>`.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Single continuous "immunogenicity score" per peptide-HLA pair, bounded ~0-1, HIGHER = MORE immunogenic (validation uses a 0.5 threshold to call immunogenic vs non). The regression target during training is a continuous immunogenic potential fit via a beta-binomial model over IEDB response rates (not a raw binary), so the output is an immunogenicity-potential estimate, not a binding/presentation %rank or IC50. Direction is CONCORDANT with our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds (both: higher = more immunogenic), so no sign flip needed when ranking.
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on >9,000 IEDB class-I T-cell immunogenicity assays (linear epitope, MHC-I, human, any disease; IEDB snapshot 2020-08-13), restricted to 9/10-mers (97.5% of instances), with a beta-binomial continuous target. Internal: 10-fold cross-validation (CNN auROC 0.85, auPR 0.81 reported on the dengue-context CV). External held-out real-world sets: (1) dengue (accuracy ~0.91 AdaBoost variant), (2) cancer neoantigens (~83% sensitivity; enriched hits in top-20/top-50 vs IEDB tool), (3) SARS-CoV-2 (68-88% recall at low precision 0.11-0.28 on a heavily imbalanced set). Benchmarked as outperforming DeepHLApan and the IEDB immunogenicity tool on precision/recall across HLA genotypes. NOTE: no splice-junction / frameshift-specific validation exists in the paper.
dossiers.integration_notes: unused

dossiers.name: IEDB Class I Immunogenicity (Calis 2013)
dossiers.admits_evidence: Model scores any amino-acid sequence: it sums position-weighted log-enrichment scores over the peptide with no reference to a source proteome. The paper states source-protein annotation was used only for redundancy reduction during training, not for scoring new peptides; the tool requires only peptide sequence + MHC allele (proteome-agnostic). Calis et al. 2013 PLoS Comput Biol, https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003266 ; IEDB reference https://tools.iedb.org/immunogenicity/reference/ . Caveat: validated ONLY on 9mers; per IEDB help, longer peptides are handled crudely (extra residues treated as if inserted after position 5 with position-5 weighting/masking), so 10-11mer splice/frameshift epitopes get a degraded score — https://tools.iedb.org/immunogenicity/help/ .
dossiers.availability: open-source / web-server + downloadable standalone (Python) + REST API; also bundled in Next-Generation IEDB T-cell Class I tool. Free for non-commercial/academic use; explicit commercial-use terms unverified.
dossiers.inputs: Required: peptide sequence (9mer for validated use; other lengths accepted but not validated) + MHC allele. The allele is used ONLY to select which anchor positions are masked (zeroed) — default masks positions 1, 2, and C-terminus; "Allele Specific" mode adds extra anchor positions for some HLA. No expression, no flanking sequence, no affinity input needed. Custom mask option requires uniform peptide length.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Single raw scalar = sum over unmasked positions of (position-importance weight x per-position amino-acid log-enrichment score). Direction: HIGHER = MORE immunogenic; score > 0 means the pMHC is more like the immunogenic training set than not, score < 0 the inverse. Not a %rank, not a probability, not calibrated — an unbounded real-valued log-enrichment sum. Rank-only interpretation is appropriate.
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on 600 immunogenic + 181 non-immunogenic murine 9mer peptides (peptide-immunization studies, redundancy-reduced by source protein). Position importance from Kullback-Leibler divergence; positions 4-6 most informative (Fisher p<0.01); large/aromatic residues (F,W,Y,I,M,K) positive, small (S) negative. Held-out performance: 3-fold cross-validation AUC = 0.65; independent Dengue-virus test sets AUC = 0.69 (mouse) and 0.61 (human). Modest discrimination — a weak-but-real baseline, not a strong ranker.
dossiers.integration_notes: Ideal reference baseline for the #736 A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment harness: it is THE canonical, most-cited immunogenicity predictor (also embedded in NetTepi), sequence-only, so it needs nothing our registry lacks (no expression/flanks). Run the standalone Python tool or REST API with allele=HLA-A*02:01 (Allele-Specific masking) over the 81 functional-scorable positives, emit the raw score, and rank alongside calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds — both are monotone "higher = more immunogenic," so no sign flip; compare by rank/enrichment (AUC, top-k), never by absolute value (Calis is uncalibrated). TWO caveats to document: (1) 89% of the registry is A*02:01, matching the allele the default masking is tuned for — good. (2) Registry epitopes are 9-11mers; Calis is validated only on 9mers and degrades 10-11mers via the "insert-after-position-5" heuristic, so stratify the harness by length (report 9mer-only vs all-lengths) to avoid attributing length-handling artifacts to true model skill. Treat it as the floor the pipeline's calibrated score must beat, not as ground truth.

dossiers.name: Repitope
dossiers.admits_evidence: Repitope is MHC-agnostic and imposes no proteome/annotation restriction: it scores any amino-acid sequence of the allowed length (MHC-I: "8- to 11-aa HLA-I-restricted" peptides; MHC-II: 11-30aa) by profiling it against a fixed reference public TCR repertoire (191,326 CDR3beta sequences), so novel-junction / intron-retention / poison-exon / frameshift 9-11mers are all admissible peptides. The authors explicitly note training "peptide data were not necessarily accompanied by the restricting MHC metadata." Paper: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6477061/ (iScience 2019, doi:10.1016/j.isci.2019.03.038). Caveat (not a proteome lock, a leakage warning): the README advises checking whether candidate peptides are already in Repitope's own datasets since predicting training peptides is not recommended - relevant because our registry positives are largely IEDB-sourced and Repitope trained on IEDB. Code: https://github.com/masato-ogishi/Repitope
dossiers.availability: open-source (R package; masato-ogishi/Repitope v3.1.7; MIT + file LICENSE). Large fragment library + feature dataframes + precomputed human immunogenicity scores hosted on Mendeley Data (v3.0.1+). A Python port is also referenced. Unverified: exact Mendeley DOI and whether prebuilt A*02:01-relevant feature tables cover all our 9-11mers.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: "Immunogenicity score" - a probabilistic estimate on [0,1] synthesized from ensembles of extremely randomized trees; interpreted as "the probability of functional T cell recognition." Direction: HIGHER = more immunogenic (same direction as our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds, so no sign flip when co-ranking). It is a raw per-peptide probability, NOT a %rank and NOT allele-conditioned. A second output, "escape potential" (max immunogenicity delta over single-AA variants; lower = more stable), is not relevant to the #736 ranking task. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6477061/
dossiers.inputs: peptideSet (peptide sequences only) + peptideLengthSet (8:11 for MHC-I). NO MHC allele input - the model is MHC-agnostic and scores peptides against a fixed public TCR repertoire, so it cannot be conditioned on A*02:01. Also requires: a precomputed featureDF (MHC-I or MHC-II specific), a fragment library (fragLib) built from public TCR clonotypes via CPP_FragmentLibrary(), the minimum featureSet (32 features MHC-I / 27 MHC-II), metadataDF, and seedSet. No TCR or expression input from the user. Prediction entrypoint: EpitopePrioritization(). Separate MHC-I and MHC-II classifiers. Source: https://github.com/masato-ogishi/Repitope/blob/master/README.md
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained/evaluated on 21,162 HLA-I and 31,693 HLA-II peptides pooled from IEDB + LANL (HIV/HCV) etc. (immunogenic fraction 32.9% HLA-I, 52.5% HLA-II), using five-times-repeated 5-fold cross-validation with strict held-out design (each peptide predicted only by models trained on sets excluding it). Reported cross-validation stability (normalized SD medians 8.3% MHC-I / 5.0% MHC-II). Independent generalization check: MHC-I tumor-associated epitopes from TANTIGEN scored higher than non-immunogenic peptides. Numeric AUROC values were not confirmed in the text I retrieved (unverified - separation shown graphically). Note: validation is standard-peptide, NOT splice/frameshift/neojunction - no non-canonical held-out set exists.
dossiers.integration_notes: Usable as an ORTHOGONAL baseline next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds: it encodes a TCR-repertoire contact-potential signal, distinct from MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor's presentation signal, and admits our non-canonical 9-11mers. Wiring: (1) our harness is Python; Repitope is R, so bridge via rpy2 or a subprocess R script calling EpitopePrioritization() over the registry peptides (or reuse the Python port if it reproduces v3.1.7). (2) Download fragLib + MHC-I featureDF from Mendeley; peptides absent from the prebuilt feature table need CPP feature recomputation (heavy). (3) Score is MHC-agnostic - fine for #736 because the harness is ~89% A*02:01 (allele ~constant), so allele-blind ranking loses little; rank descending, same direction as our log-odds. (4) CRITICAL leakage screen before any enrichment claim: Repitope trained on IEDB immunogenicity data and our registry positives are largely IEDB-recovered - compute exact peptide overlap against Repitope's datasets and exclude/flag overlapping positives, or the enrichment is inflated by memorization (the README's own caveat). (5) Report it as a reference/secondary ranker, not the primary; it cannot exploit our A*02:01 restriction or expression/flank context. Frameshift + long poison-exon peptides could additionally be routed through the MHC-II classifier if 11-30mers arise.

dossiers.name: PRIME (Schmidt et al. 2021, Cell Reports Medicine) — labeled "iPred" in brief; actual name is PRIME v1.0
dossiers.admits_evidence: PRIME takes an arbitrary peptide string + HLA-I allele as input (peptides 8-14 aa); the GitHub README/usage shows a plain peptide list, no proteome/annotation lookup, so any sequence including splice/frameshift-derived non-canonical peptides can be scored. Caveat: the training set is canonical antigens + point-mutation neoepitopes (pathogen/cancer-testis n~1,629 + cancer mutations n~3,329 + random negatives), so scoring frameshift/splice peptides is an out-of-distribution extrapolation, not a validated regime. Sources: https://github.com/GfellerLab/PRIME ; https://www.cell.com/cell-reports-medicine/fulltext/S2666-3791(21)00005-7
dossiers.availability: open-source (Perl/CLI wrapper, GfellerLab/PRIME) + web server (prime.gfellerlab.org); free for academic/non-commercial, for-profit use requires a paid license via Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research. Hard dependency on MixMHCpred (v3.0+, same lab, same license terms) which must be installed and on PATH.
dossiers.inputs: Required: peptide sequence (8-14 aa) + one HLA-I allele (e.g. A0201). No expression, no flanking sequence, no structure. External dependency: MixMHCpred v3.0+ installed locally (PRIME calls it to get the -log(%rank) binding term). Pan-allele coverage inherited from MixMHCpred's supported HLA-I list (A*02:01 fully supported). PRIME1.0 (the 2021 reference) vs PRIME2.0/2.1 (Cell Systems 2022, later versions) differ in method and allele breadth — see output_score_semantics.
dossiers.integration_notes: DIRECTION FLIP REQUIRED: PRIME emits %rank where LOWER = more immunogenic, whereas our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds is higher = more immunogenic. To co-rank, invert (use -%rank or -log(%rank), the same transform PRIME uses internally on MixMHCpred). NAME/METHOD MISMATCH IN BRIEF: the task calls this 'iPred / gradient-boosted-tree'; the actual Cell Reports Medicine 2021 model is PRIME v1.0, a LOGISTIC REGRESSION over [MixMHCpred -log(%rank)] + [amino-acid frequencies at TCR-facing/non-anchor positions]. PRIME2.0 (Cell Systems 2022) swapped to a small fully-connected NN (5 hidden nodes) — still no gradient-boosted trees. If the harness intends a true GBT immunogenicity baseline, PredIG (XGBoost, github.com/BSC-CNS-EAPM/PredIG) or a separate 'iPred' is the tool, not this paper. INTEGRATION COST: needs MixMHCpred install (Perl/binary) alongside our Python stack; both are CLI, wrap as a subprocess step post-MHCflurry. A*02:01-only restriction of #736 is fully within support. Score peptides directly (no proteome lookup) so splice/frameshift 9-11mers pass through unchanged. Because it is presentation+TCR combined (like calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds sits post-presentation), treat it as a complementary immunogenicity ranker, not an orthogonal-feature baseline.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Primary output is a %rank: the fraction of random human-proteome peptides scoring >= the query, normalized like MixMHCpred. LOWER %rank = MORE immunogenic (inverse direction vs our log-odds). A raw combined score is also emitted. Not a calibrated probability. PRIME1.0 (2021 reference) = logistic-regression combined score; PRIME2.0/2.1 = NN, same %rank semantics/direction.
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on peptides experimentally tested for CD8+ T-cell response: pathogen/cancer-testis antigens (~1,629) + cancer point-mutation neoepitopes (~3,329) + random negatives; features = MixMHCpred binding + per-position amino-acid frequencies. Validated by (a) cross-validation AUC showing improved neo-epitope prioritization over HLA-binding predictors alone, (b) an independent held-out mouse neopeptide dataset experimentally assayed for immune response, and (c) a cancer-genomics analysis where recurrent mutations predicted immunogenic were less frequent across patients — interpreted as immunoediting evidence. All positives/held-out are canonical or point-mutation neoepitopes; no splice/frameshift/intron-retention peptides in training or validation (relevant caveat for #736). Sources: https://www.cell.com/cell-reports-medicine/fulltext/S2666-3791(21)00005-7 ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9811684/

dossiers.name: DeepHLApan
dossiers.admits_evidence: DeepHLApan scores any (peptide, HLA) pair supplied in a CSV with columns "Annotation,HLA,peptide" — the HLA is encoded as a 34-aa pseudo-sequence (residues within 4.0 A of the peptide) concatenated with the raw peptide and padded to 49 aa, with no lookup against a reference proteome, so the input is a free amino-acid string. The paper explicitly runs it on "8-11 mer peptides covering the mutations" from somatic-variant data (a neoantigen use case), confirming arbitrary mutation-derived sequences are admitted. A frameshift/intron-retention/poison-exon peptide is just such an arbitrary string and is accepted identically; the only real gate is that the HLA must be one of the class-I A/B/C alleles the binding model was trained on (81 alleles from IEDB; A*02:01 is included). Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6838785/ and repo README https://github.com/jiujiezz/deephlapan (paper: Wu et al., Front. Immunol. 2019, doi:10.3389/fimmu.2019.02559).
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python/Keras-TensorFlow, GPL-2.0) + docker + web-server
dossiers.availability_url: https://github.com/jiujiezz/deephlapan (mirror/updated: https://github.com/zjupgx/deephlapan); docker biopharm/deephlapan:v1.1; web server http://biopharm.zju.edu.cn/deephlapan (webserver liveness unverified)
dossiers.inputs: CSV with header "Annotation,HLA,peptide" (or CLI -P peptide -H HLA). Peptide = raw amino-acid string; paper trains binding on 8-15mers and evaluates neoantigens on 8-11mers, so our 9-11mers are in range. HLA in colon notation but WITHOUT the star, e.g. "HLA-A02:01" (not "HLA-A*02:01") — format-normalization needed at the harness boundary. Class-I A/B/C only; allele must be within the ~81 IEDB-trained alleles. NO expression, NO flanking-sequence, NO cleavage/processing inputs — peptide+allele only.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two independent 0-1 sigmoid scores per (peptide,HLA) pair, both higher=better. (1) binding score = probability the peptide is presented by the HLA; (2) immunogenic(ity) score = probability the pHLA elicits T-cell activation. For our A*02:01 ranking harness the immunogenicity score is the directly comparable analogue of calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds; note it is a bounded probability (0-1), NOT a log-odds, so to rank alongside our log-odds either rank on the raw probability or transform via logit(p). The authors' own neoantigen recipe: drop pairs with immunogenic score <0.5, then rank survivors by binding score — i.e. immunogenicity used as a gate and binding as the fine rank; for a pure enrichment benchmark you can instead rank continuously on the immunogenicity score.
dossiers.validation_summary: Two separately trained GRU/bidirectional-RNN models. Binding model: 437,077 HLA-peptide pairs (280,525 measured + 156,552 synthetic pseudo-pairs) over 81 IEDB alleles; immunogenicity model: 32,785 IEDB pairs (7,212 immunogenic-labeled). Trained with five-fold cross-validation (checkpoint on validation-accuracy improvement). External evaluation by AUC: IEDB weekly-benchmark comparison vs 12 tools (best on 6/11 datasets), an independent mass-spec presentation dataset (Mei et al., 15 sub-datasets), and a neoantigen set (Kosaloglu-Yalcin et al.) where it improved precision/recall. Caveat for #736: the immunogenicity training labels are IEDB canonical/point-mutation epitopes (heavily A*02:01), NOT splice-junction neoantigens, so it is an out-of-domain baseline — exactly the reference-only role #736 needs. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6838785/
dossiers.integration_notes: Strong fit as an external baseline column next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds on the A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment harness, because (a) it is genuinely open-source (GPL-2.0) and dockerized — reproducible, no web-server-only lock-in — and (b) it emits an explicit immunogenicity score, the same construct we rank on, unlike affinity-only tools. Wiring: it consumes peptide+allele only (no expression/flank), so feed the same 9-11mer + A*02:01 rows we send MHCflurry; NO extra features to compute. Two adapters needed: (1) allele-string normalization "HLA-A*02:01" -> "HLA-A02:01" for its CSV; (2) score is a bounded 0-1 probability, so for rank comparability either rank on raw p or apply logit(p) — do NOT treat it as a log-odds. Use the continuous immunogenicity score for enrichment/AUC-style ranking rather than its published <0.5 gate + binding-rank recipe (the gate throws away rank information we need on a tiny positive set). GPL-2.0 is copyleft: fine for internal benchmarking, but do not vendor its code into our (presumably permissively-licensed) pipeline — call it as an external/docker tool. Environment caveat: repo pins CUDA 9 / cuDNN 7 era Keras-TensorFlow, which will not run on our macOS arm64 CPU core cleanly — run it via the provided docker image or a pinned legacy TF env on the GPU-revival path, not in-process. Domain caveat to state in the deck: its immunogenicity model is trained on IEDB canonical epitopes, so on splice-junction non-canonical peptides it is an out-of-distribution reference point, which is appropriate for a reference-only/adaptable baseline but must not be sold as calibrated on our epitope class.

dossiers.name: DeepNeo (DeepNeo-v2)
dossiers.admits_evidence: The paper's "protein sequence" input mode explicitly targets non-canonical epitopes: DeepNeo "identifies potential neoepitopes from a novel sequence usually derived by frameshift mutations or aberrant splicing." So it accepts arbitrary peptide sequences (not proteome-locked) — it scores any pMHC pair you submit. CAVEAT: the scorer only accepts fixed lengths — 9-mers for MHC-I and 15-mers for MHC-II — so our 10/11-mer splice epitopes are NOT directly scoreable on the MHC-I head without truncation/registration, even though frameshift/splice ORIGIN is explicitly in scope. Source: Kim et al., NAR 2023, 51(W1):W134-W140, https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/51/W1/W134/7127215 and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10320182/ (DOI 10.1093/nar/gkad275).
dossiers.availability: web-server-only (https://deepneo.net, free, no login). Code framework is Keras 2.4.0 / Python 3.7 per paper, but the GitHub repo referenced in search results (github.com/kaistomics/DeepNeo) currently returns HTTP 404 — only the unrelated kaistomics/DeepNeo-BCR remains — so open-source local execution is UNVERIFIED/effectively unavailable as of this check.
dossiers.availability_url: https://deepneo.net (web server); paper: https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/51/W1/W134/7127215 ; code repo github.com/kaistomics/DeepNeo returns 404 (unverified/removed)
dossiers.inputs: Required: (1) peptide — 9-mer for MHC-I, 15-mer for MHC-II (fixed length only); or a protein FASTA in "protein mode" for de novo neoepitope scanning; (2) HLA allele at 4-digit resolution, restricted to HLA-A, -B, -DQB1, -DRB1 (human) plus most mouse alleles — NOTE: no HLA-C support. No expression, flank, or processing inputs. Two input modes: peptide list (tab-delimited peptide+HLA) or protein FASTA with HLA listed separately. Species: human + mouse. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10320182/
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two independent scores in [0,1], higher = more likely: (1) DeepNeo-mhc binding/presentation score, (2) DeepNeo-tcr T-cell reactivity score. Direction: higher = more immunogenic. Decision rule: a pMHC is called immunogenic only if BOTH scores > 0.5 (binding-positive but reactivity-below-0.5 = presented-but-nonimmunogenic). These are raw sigmoid probabilities, NOT %rank and NOT calibrated log-odds. For our A*02:01 ranking harness the natural single ranker is the DeepNeo-tcr reactivity score (optionally gated on binding>0.5). Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10320182/
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on IEDB (snapshot 2022-08-30): MHC-I binding n=340,987 (75% pos), MHC-II binding n=106,908 (57.2% pos), TCR-I reactivity n=19,149 (42.6% pos), TCR-II n=12,913 (42.4% pos). Three-fold cross-validation with random train/test splits (NOT allele- or source-disjoint holdout — leakage risk for our purpose). Reported: MHC-I ROC-AUC 0.969, MHC-II 0.882; TCR-I ROC-AUC 0.805, TCR-II 0.826. External validation: in-vitro immunogenicity assays (MHC-I) + 812 independent pMHC-II immunogenicity measurements. Claims to outperform PRIME, NetMHCpan, MixMHCpred. No splice/frameshift-specific validation set reported. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10320182/
dossiers.integration_notes: Good conceptual baseline: DeepNeo-tcr is a presentation-plus-reactivity predictor in the same family as our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds (post-presentation immunogenicity layer), so it maps cleanly onto a NO-SCALAR enrichment/ranking comparison on A*02:01 — rank our positives by DeepNeo reactivity score and compare AUROC/enrichment against calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds. BUT four integration frictions: (1) LENGTH LOCK — MHC-I head is 9-mer-only, so 10/11-mer registry positives cannot be scored natively; you must either restrict the harness to the 9-mer subset (shrinks an already-tiny n=81) or define a fixed truncation/sliding-window registration, which is a methods decision to document. (2) NO API — web-server-only (deepneo.net) and the GitHub repo is 404, so scoring is manual/batch-through-the-UI; no drop-in Python call next to MHCflurry unless authors restore code. (3) Score is a raw [0,1] sigmoid, not calibrated log-odds — fine for ranking (direction: higher=better) but not directly comparable in magnitude; rank-normalize before plotting alongside our log-odds. (4) Training-data leakage caveat — IEDB-2022 with random (non-source-disjoint) CV means our IEDB-recovered splice registry entries may overlap DeepNeo's training data; treat DeepNeo as a reference-only baseline, not an independent oracle, and flag any registry positive with an IEDB provenance date < 2022-08-30. License is CC-BY-NC (non-commercial) — acceptable for an academic benchmark. Net: usable as a comparator column but requires a documented length-handling rule and a leakage disclaimer; not a zero-effort drop-in.

dossiers.name: BigMHC EL
dossiers.admits_evidence: BigMHC scores on peptide-sequence + allele-name alone with no proteome/annotation lookup: the README states it does "fuzzy string matching" on allele names and "will make predictions even if given nonsense or MHC-II input" — i.e. no proteome lock, any amino-acid string is scored. Base peptide window is length 8 (Class-I 8-11mers), covering our 9-11mer IR/poison-exon/novel-junction/frameshift peptides. Evidence: https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc (README input spec + "nonsense" note) and paper https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6 . Caveat: EL scores presentation likelihood, not immunogenicity, and there is no special-casing for frameshift/junction origin — it treats them as ordinary peptide strings (which is exactly what we want for a sequence-only baseline).
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python / PyTorch), GitHub KarchinLab/bigmhc, model weights bundled (~5 GB clone); runs CPU or GPU (predict.py -d cpu). License is NON-commercial: "BigMHC Academic License" (free for teaching/research at non-profits; commercial use requires contacting the licensor).
dossiers.inputs: A CSV with a peptide column (required) and an MHC-I allele-name column (optional; if omitted, one allele passed as an argument). Columns are zero-indexed. Allele names are fuzzy-matched, so "HLA-A*02:01", "A*02:01", "HLAA0201", "A0201" are all accepted/equivalent — ideal for pinning our A*02:01 harness. NO expression value and NO flanking sequence are required (unlike NetMHCpan/MHCflurry EL, which optionally take flanks). Supported lengths: Class-I 8-11mers (base window length 8); exact upper length cap is unverified in the docs.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Per (peptide, allele) RAW presentation score in [0,1] (eluted-ligand presentation likelihood), written to <input>.prd. Higher = more likely presented. It is a raw probability-like scalar, NOT a %rank and NOT calibrated to our immunogenicity log-odds; direction is monotone-higher-is-better, so it drops straight into a descending-rank / enrichment harness. Note: EL = presentation (comparable to our MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor layer), distinct from BigMHC IM which transfer-learns immunogenicity — for a direct comparator to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds, IM is the closer sibling, but EL is a valid presentation baseline.
dossiers.validation_summary: Ensemble of 7 pan-allelic deep neural networks trained on peptide-MHC mass-spec eluted-ligand (EL) data. Authors report presentation performance on a test set of 45,409 MHC ligands among 900,592 random negatives: AUROC = 0.9733, AUPRC = 0.8779, reported to beat NetMHCpan-4.1 and other state-of-the-art classifiers, and to hold up when stratified by MHC allele and by peptide length. (The neoepitope-immunogenicity ranking / PPV validation applies to BigMHC IM, the transfer-learned head, not to EL.) Exact held-out split design (allele-holdout vs peptide-holdout) is unverified beyond the stratified-by-allele/length statement. Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6 and preprint https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690v2 .
dossiers.integration_notes: Wire as: build a 2-col CSV (peptide, "HLA-A*02:01") from the 81 functional-scorable / A*02:01-heavy registry rows, run `python predict.py -i=in.csv -m=el -d="cpu"`, read the EL column from in.csv.prd. CPU path (-d cpu) fits the current $0-budget local-only posture; ~5 GB weights are vendored in the repo. In the ranking/enrichment harness, rank peptides by EL descending alongside calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds and MHCflurry presentation_score; because EL is presentation (not immunogenicity) it is a fair *presentation-layer* baseline and a useful ablation showing how much our log-odds adds on top of raw presentation. For a like-for-like immunogenicity comparator also pull BigMHC IM (-m=im). Two watch-items: (1) LICENSE is non-commercial academic only — fine for our research benchmark, but flag before any commercial redistribution/derived-model shipping; (2) confirm the max peptide length accepted for our 11mers on a smoke test since the docs don't state an explicit cap. No proteome/annotation input needed, so splice/frameshift peptides pass through unmodified.

dossiers.name: BigMHC IM
dossiers.admits_evidence: The Karchin Lab repo README explicitly states BigMHC "will make predictions even if given nonsense or MHC-II input, as it will find the nearest valid MHC name to the provided invalid allele name" — inference consumes only the raw peptide string + an MHC pseudo-sequence, with no proteome lookup or annotation constraint, so any 8-11mer (intron-retention, poison-exon, novel-junction, frameshift) is scored on its sequence alone. https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc (unverified whether it ever hard-checks the 20-AA alphabet). Caveat: the IM transfer-learning NEGATIVES include 9-mers randomly sampled from the human proteome, but that is a training-set construction detail, not an inference-time proteome gate. https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690v2.full
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python/PyTorch, KarchinLab/bigmhc); ~5GB clone incl. weights; runs CPU or GPU
dossiers.inputs: A CSV with (at minimum) a peptide column and optionally an MHC-I allele column; column indices passed via -p / -a flags (zero-indexed). Allele name is fuzzy-matched (accepts HLA-A*02:01, A*02:01, HLAA0201, A0201 all equivalently; A*02:01 is explicitly supported). NO expression value and NO flanking sequence required (unlike MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor's optional flanks). Peptide length 8-11mers (the paper restricted evaluated peptides to len 8-11). Source: repo README + biorxiv. https://github.com/KarchinLab/bigmhc ; https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690v2.full
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Per-(peptide, allele) IMMUNOGENICITY score written to a CSV column (.prd output). It is an immune-response probability (sigmoid ensemble output of 7 DNNs), NOT a presentation/binding score — that is the separate BigMHC EL head. Direction: HIGHER = more immunogenic (more likely to elicit an antigen-specific T-cell response); rank descending. Exact [0,1] calibration is unverified in docs but it is a per-peptide probability-like score suitable for ranking. This sits at the SAME conceptual layer as our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds (an immunogenicity-layer score consumed post-MHCflurry presentation).
dossiers.validation_summary: BigMHC EL (presentation head) trained on MS eluted-ligand pMHC data; test set 45,409 MHC ligands vs 900,592 random negatives -> AUROC 0.9733, AUPRC 0.8779, beating NetMHCpan-4.1, stratified by HLA supertype + peptide length. BigMHC IM is TRANSFER-LEARNED (fine-tuned from EL) on antigen-specific immune-response assay data: PRIME + TESLA datasets (viral antigens, cancer-testis antigens, neoepitopes, plus proteome-sampled 9mer negatives). Immunogenicity validation was on held-out MANAFEST neoepitope data (167 immunogenic / 672 non-immunogenic), scored by PPVn (positive predictive value among top-n) — the precision-at-top metric that matches a ranking/enrichment benchmark. Reported result: after transfer learning BigMHC yields significantly higher precision than 7 state-of-the-art immunogenicity models. Sources: https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.08.29.505690v2.full ; https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00694-6
dossiers.integration_notes: Strong baseline for the #736 A*02:01 no-scalar ranking/enrichment harness. (1) It only needs the peptide string + "HLA-A*02:01" — no expression, no flanks — so our non-canonical 9-11mers wire in unchanged; feed the registry's 81 functional-scorable positives (89% A*02:01) as a peptide CSV and rank by the IM column. (2) Its PPVn / top-n precision framing is EXACTLY the metric family #736 should adopt (precision-at-top / enrichment), so BigMHC IM is both a baseline predictor AND a template for the eval metric. (3) Layer-matched to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds: both are post-presentation immunogenicity scores, so compare them head-to-head on the same A*02:01 ranking (Spearman of ranks + PPV@k + enrichment vs the n=1 hard negative). (4) BigMHC EL can double as a presentation cross-check against our MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor. CAVEATS to flag on the board: (a) license is the custom "BigMHC Academic License" (JHU/Karchin), NON-COMMERCIAL/academic only — fine for an open research benchmark, but NOT an OSI license; commercial use needs a separate grant, so note it in the benchmark's licensing manifest. (b) ~5GB clone (bundled weights); PyTorch ensemble of 7 nets — plan the CPU-only keep-alive runtime. (c) MANAFEST/PRIME/TESLA training set OVERLAP is a leakage risk: any registry positive drawn from PRIME/TESLA/MANAFEST is in-distribution for BigMHC IM and would inflate it vs our score — audit provenance and exclude/mark overlapping rows before comparing. (d) No published splice/frameshift-specific validation (paper never mentions splice or frameshift); admission is by sequence-only architecture, so #736 would itself be the first non-canonical stress test — state that explicitly.

dossiers.name: SNAF-T (DeepImmuno-CNN)
dossiers.admits_evidence: DeepImmuno-CNN scores ARBITRARY peptide+HLA pairs with no proteome restriction: its input is a raw peptide string + HLA allele, encoded via physicochemical (AAindex) + HLA paratope/pseudo-sequence features, so any sequence is admissible (github.com/frankligy/DeepImmuno; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8135853/). SNAF's T-antigen module upstream does in-silico translation of non-canonical tumor splice junctions (cassette exon, intron retention, novel/alt splice site) and feeds the resulting peptides to DeepImmuno (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11517820/; SNAF, Sci Transl Med, doi:10.1126/scitranslmed.ade2886). CAVEAT: DeepImmuno-CNN is length-LOCKED to 9- and 10-mers only ("Deepimmuno-CNN only supports peptides in the length of 9 and 10", DeepImmuno FAQ) - our registry's 11-mers cannot be scored without truncation/registration. Frameshift peptides are admissible only insofar as SNAF's junction translation emits them; no dedicated frameshift-ORF handling is documented as verified.
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python3; DeepImmuno-CNN MIT, SNAF MIT)
dossiers.availability_url: https://github.com/frankligy/DeepImmuno (MIT; web server https://deepimmuno.research.cchmc.org) ; https://github.com/frankligy/SNAF (MIT; PyPI https://pypi.org/project/SNAF/ ; Zenodo https://zenodo.org/records/10252900 ; docs https://snaf.readthedocs.io)
dossiers.inputs: DeepImmuno-CNN (the actual immunogenicity scorer we would baseline): peptide sequence (9- or 10-mer ONLY) + a single 4-digit HLA class I allele (e.g. HLA-A*0201). No flanks, no expression, no MHC-binding prefilter required at this layer. CLI: `python3 deepimmuno-cnn.py --mode single --epitope HPPLMNVER --hla HLA-A*0201` or `--mode multiple` with a CSV of (peptide,HLA). Full SNAF-T pipeline (if run end-to-end): tumor RNA-Seq BAMs + per-sample HLA genotype; it derives junctions, translates peptides, runs netMHCpan OR MHCflurry for binding, then DeepImmuno for immunogenicity. For a #736 baseline you only need the DeepImmuno-CNN layer, fed peptide+A*02:01.
dossiers.integration_notes: This is the closest architectural analog to our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds: both sit as a post-MHC, T-cell-recognition immunogenicity layer (ours post-MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor; DeepImmuno post-netMHCpan/MHCflurry in SNAF). Wire it as a peer scorer, NOT a replacement. Concrete harness steps: (1) install DeepImmuno-CNN standalone (avoid the full SNAF BAM pipeline - we only need the scorer); note the pinned TensorFlow==2.3.0 / numpy==1.18.5 / pandas==1.1.1 deps are old - isolate in its own venv/conda env, do NOT pollute the snakemake env. (2) For each registry A*02:01 positive, emit (peptide, "HLA-A*0201") rows; DROP or explicitly flag the 11-mers (length-locked to 9/10) - decide truncation policy up front and record it, since ~a slice of the registry is 11-mers. (3) Run `--mode multiple`, collect the [0,1] immunogenicity score, rank descending (higher = more immunogenic, same direction as our log-odds). (4) Compare rank-enrichment of registry positives head-to-head with calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds using the no-scalar ranking/enrichment framing (AUROC/top-k enrichment on A*02:01), not a calibrated specificity metric. Caveat for interpretation: DeepImmuno's score is a beta-binomial recognition confidence, uncalibrated across alleles - fine here because #736 is fixed to A*02:01 (a well-covered, primary DeepImmuno test allele). Both scores are direction-aligned so no sign flip needed.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Continuous immunogenicity score in [0,1] from a beta-binomial probabilistic model (DeepImmuno-CNN); HIGHER = more immunogenic. Authors use a 0.5 threshold for binary immunogenic/non-immunogenic calls in benchmarks. It is a T-cell-recognition/immunogenicity confidence, NOT an MHC-binding affinity or presentation score (binding is handled separately upstream by netMHCpan/MHCflurry). Direction matches our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds (higher = more immunogenic). Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8135853/ , https://academic.oup.com/bib/article/22/6/bbab160/6261914
dossiers.validation_summary: DeepImmuno-CNN: benchmarked against DeepHLApan and IEDB immunogenicity tool on held-out real-world sets (dengue, cancer neoantigen, SARS-CoV-2), reporting improved precision/recall/AUC across HLA genotypes; CNN chosen over ElasticNet/KNN/SVM/RF/AdaBoost/ResNet/GNN (Briefings in Bioinformatics 2021, bbab160; PMC8135853). SNAF-T (the splice-junction application, Sci Transl Med 2024, doi:10.1126/scitranslmed.ade2886; PMC11517820): validated splice neoantigens by (a) MS immunopeptidome detection - 27/36 tested peptides MS-detected, 11 high-confidence, against the Bassani-Sternberg melanoma cohort (n=24); (b) melanoma ICB outcome - neoantigen burden associated with survival in the Van Allen anti-CTLA-4 cohort (n=39); (c) functional T-cell reactivity - 5 shared splice neoantigens induced IFNy responses comparable to FLU/HCMV positive controls. Held-out design is cross-cohort (train on IEDB-style assay data; test on independent tumor/viral cohorts), not a splice-specific immunogenicity held-out benchmark - so it corroborates but does not itself constitute the powered splice-immunogenicity benchmark #736 targets.

dossiers.name: DeepImmuno (DeepImmuno-CNN)
dossiers.admits_evidence: DeepImmuno-CNN takes only a raw peptide sequence + an HLA allele; it requires no proteome annotation, transcript, gene, or flanking context, so it will score any arbitrary/non-canonical peptide (intron-retention, poison-exon, novel-junction, frameshift) as long as the peptide is a 9mer or 10mer. The paper: "Currently, DeepImmuno-CNN only supports peptides in the length of 9 and 10" — 11mers in our registry cannot be scored. GitHub README shows the minimal input: `AAAAAAAAA,HLA-A*0201`.
