Sources: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8135853/ ; https://github.com/frankligy/DeepImmuno (CAVEAT: 9/10mer-only length cap; web-server FAQ also flags a "Caveat" label for alleles with sparse training evidence).
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python 3 / TensorFlow 2.3, MIT license) + free public web server; also on tamarind.bio wrapper
dossiers.inputs: Two fields per row: (1) peptide sequence, restricted to length 9 or 10 (11mers unsupported — the paper explicitly retains only 9/10mers); (2) an HLA class-I allele in 4-digit form, e.g. HLA-A*0201 (asterisk escaped on CLI as HLA-A\*0201). MHC is encoded via a pseudo-sequence derived from the MHCflurry allele-sequence table, so "any HLA-A/-B locus protein of known sequence" is nominally accepted. NO expression, abundance, or flanking-sequence inputs are used or required. CLI: `python3 deepimmuno-cnn.py --mode single --epitope HPPLMNVER --hla HLA-A*0201`, plus a `multiple`/CSV batch mode. Unverified: whether HLA-C alleles are reliably covered (examples lean A/B; web FAQ warns results are unreliable for alleles sparse in training).
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Single continuous "immunogenicity" score in [0,1]; HIGHER = more immunogenic (greater confidence of a T-cell response). It is NOT an MHC-binding score and NOT a plain probability — the training target is a beta-binomial-derived continuous "immunogenic potential" that up/down-weights each pMHC pair by the strength of experimental evidence (e.g. tested in 40 subjects vs 6). The authors use 0.5 as a binary-classification cutoff in benchmarks but describe the value as reflecting confidence given experimental evidence, not absolute immunogenicity. Direction matches our ranker (higher = more immunogenic), so it is rank-comparable for an enrichment/ranking harness after rank-normalization. Sources: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8135853/ ; https://deepimmuno.research.cchmc.org/learn_more
dossiers.validation_summary: Trained on 8,971 IEDB MHC-I T-cell-assay instances (4,059 immunogenic positives / 4,912 negatives), 9/10mers, encoded with a PCA-reduced AAindex1 physicochemical space (566 features) and a beta-binomial confidence-weighted target. Internal: 10-fold cross-validation auROC ~0.85, auPR ~0.81. External held-out generalization on three independent sets: a dengue-virus set (~408 instances), the TESLA tumor-neoantigen set (~608 instances; DeepImmuno recovered 29/35 validated immunogenic neoantigens vs IEDB 63% and DeepHLApan 34%), and a SARS-CoV-2 peptide set (~100). Comparators: IEDB "immunogenicity" position-weighted model and DeepHLApan (RNN). Note: TESLA/neoantigen validation is canonical point-mutation neoantigens, NOT splice-junction/frameshift peptides, so its splice-neoepitope transfer is unvalidated. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8135853/
dossiers.integration_notes: Strong conceptual match: like our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds, DeepImmuno scores IMMUNOGENICITY (post-binding) rather than presentation/affinity, so it sits at the same layer of the harness and is a legitimate independent baseline (different training signal, different encoding). A*02:01 is the single MOST abundant allele in its training data, so the 89%-A*02:01 registry is close to its sweet spot — good for the #736 A*02:01 ranking/enrichment benchmark. Wiring: MIT-licensed pure-Python/TF2.3 CLI, run locally in a throwaway venv (TF2.3 is old — pin/containerize; may conflict with the pipeline's modern torch/MHCflurry env, so keep it isolated). Feed each registry peptide as `<peptide>,HLA-A*0201`; emit its [0,1] score as a new column beside calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds; rank-normalize both (spearman / top-k enrichment) since one is 0-1 and the other is log-odds — direction already agrees (higher = more immunogenic). KEY CAVEAT for #736: the 9/10mer length cap means every 11mer in the registry is unscoreable by DeepImmuno — report DeepImmuno enrichment on the 9/10mer subset only and note the coverage gap, or the comparison is unfair to it. Do NOT use its 0.5 cutoff as a specificity threshold (our n=1 true-negative can't calibrate that anyway) — use it purely as a ranker.

dossiers.name: IRIS (Isoform peptides from RNA splicing for Immunotherapy target Screening)
dossiers.admits_evidence: IRIS is BUILT for splice-junction peptides but defaults to proteome-locked translation, with opt-in flags for arbitrary/frameshift ORFs. Paper (PMC10214192): IRIS "translates SJ sequences into peptide sequences using annotated reading frames from the UniProtKB database" — i.e. the default and the entire validated proof-of-concept are annotation-guided. However the CLI (github.com/Xinglab/IRIS README, `translate`/`predict` commands) exposes `--all-reading-frames`, `--ignore-annotation`, and `--remove-early-stop`, which enable non-canonical / novel-ORF / frameshift translation of the junction sequence. So it CAN emit arbitrary non-canonical peptides (unlike a pure proteome-lookup tool), but the frameshift path is off-default and was NOT the validated configuration. URLs: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10214192/ ; https://github.com/Xinglab/IRIS
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python 2.7 + Python 3 + shell, Snakemake workflow), GitHub Xinglab/IRIS; LICENSE is BSD-2-Clause, copyright UCLA. Heavyweight to stand up: requires the ~400GB IRIS_data reference bundle and a separate IEDB MHC-I tools install (mhc_i v2.15.5). Caveat (unverified): a WebFetch of the LICENSE surfaced an "academic/non-profit only, commercial must contact UCLA TDG" restriction that is inconsistent with a plain BSD-2-Clause header — treat the exact usage terms as unverified and confirm against the raw LICENSE before assuming permissive reuse.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Peptide-level score is a MEDIAN IC50 BINDING AFFINITY in nM, produced by IEDB MHC-I "recommended" mode (v2.15.5) which runs multiple tools and IRIS summarizes as the median. Direction is INVERSE: lower IC50 = stronger predicted binding = better target; the default positivity cut is median(IC50) < 500 nM. This is AFFINITY only — not presentation (unlike our MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor) and NOT a T-cell immunogenicity score; IRIS has no immunogenicity model. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10214192/
dossiers.inputs: Pipeline-level (not a peptide scorer): RNA-seq FASTQs or rMATS AS matrices, reference genome (GTF+FASTA), HLA type (seq2HLA-detected or supplied), plus tumor/normal reference panels for tumor-specificity screening; optional MS immunopeptidomics data, gene-expression matrices, mappability files. For the epitope step specifically: AS-derived junction peptides + HLA allele(s) + epitope length list (`-e EPITOPE_LEN_LIST`, typically 8-11mers). Expression/tumor-specificity filtering is upstream of the binding call.
dossiers.integration_notes: Do NOT wire IRIS in as a live peptide scorer next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds — it is a discovery PIPELINE (rMATS→translate→IEDB→tumor-specificity tiers), not a per-peptide function, and its only peptide-level number is IEDB median IC50, which we can compute directly (or already approximate via MHCflurry) without the 400GB bundle + IEDB + py2.7 stack. Three concrete uses in the #736 A*02:01 harness: (1) treat IEDB-recommended median IC50 (<500nM) as an AFFINITY BASELINE ranker to sit beside our presentation/log-odds scores — but note it is affinity-only and off-thesis for a presentation/immunogenicity benchmark, so label it a weak/reference baseline; (2) IRIS is the canonical splice→peptide TRANSLATION reference — mirror its `--all-reading-frames`/`--ignore-annotation` handling to justify our own frameshift/poison-exon translation choices; (3) its immunopeptidomics-validated target set (493 presented peptides; see below) is a candidate POSITIVE feeder for our registry, subject to the A*02:01 restriction. Direction is inverse (lower IC50 better) — invert before combining with our higher-is-better log-odds.
dossiers.validation_summary: Two-tier validation in Pan/Phillips/Zhang et al., PNAS 2023;120(21):e2221116120. (1) Immunopeptidomics proof-of-concept: integrating transcriptomics + MS, 493 IRIS-predicted AS-derived peptides were detected as HLA-presented across three cell lines (JeKo-1 n=230, B-LCL-S1 n=178, B-LCL-S2 n=85) at 5% FDR — this is PRESENTATION validation, not immunogenicity. (2) Functional T-cell validation: 7 TCRs transduced into human PBMCs showed high activity against individual IRIS-predicted epitopes, and one selected TCR showed efficient cytotoxicity against target-peptide-expressing cells (live-cell imaging). No held-out scalar-calibration or AUROC-style specificity benchmark; validation is enrichment/hit-rate + case-study functional confirmation, which aligns with #736's no-scalar ranking/enrichment posture. Source: https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2221116120 (full text https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10214192/)

dossiers.name: Frameshift-indel immunogenicity evidence (Turajlic/Litchfield; NMD-escape)
dossiers.availability: open-source (NMD-escape annotation code, R/Python; two repos) — but NO per-peptide immunogenicity scorer is released
dossiers.output_score_semantics: NOT a per-peptide immunogenicity scalar. Two usable outputs, both coarser than what a ranking harness consumes: (1) a per-variant CATEGORICAL NMD-escape annotation from the released tool — a frameshift variant's premature stop is flagged escape vs degraded; escape => higher expected antigen expression/immunogenicity (direction: escape = MORE immunogenic). (2) A CLASS-LEVEL prior from the Turajlic analysis — frameshift/indel-derived neoepitopes are ~9x enriched for mutant-specific MHC binding vs nsSNV-derived (3x for high-affinity binders); direction: frameshift > SNV. Neither is a %rank, probability, or continuous per-peptide log-odds. Unverified whether the released code emits any continuous score at all.
dossiers.admits_evidence: Turajlic et al., Lancet Oncol 2017 (PubMed 28694034): "neoantigens derived from indel mutations were nine times enriched for mutant specific binding, as compared with non-synonymous SNV derived neoantigens... enrichment of indel mutations for high-affinity binders was three times that of non-synonymous SNV"; 5777 TCGA solid tumours / 19 cancer types; frameshift-indel count associated with checkpoint-inhibitor response across three melanoma cohorts (p=4.7e-4). Litchfield/Reading et al., Nat Commun 2020 11:3800 (s41467-020-17526-5): fs-indels escaping NMD are enriched and NMD-escape fs-indel load associates with ICB clinical benefit. URLs: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28694034/ ; https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-17526-5 ; https://github.com/kevlitchfield1/NMD-escape
dossiers.inputs: Released NMD-escape tool: a frameshift VARIANT (HGVSp / VEP annotation), the transcript CDS structure (CDS bed + exon-junction coordinates), and the position of the premature termination codon. Outputs NMD-region boundaries + a per-variant escape/no-escape annotation. It requires NO MHC allele and NO peptide — it works purely on transcript/exon geometry. The Turajlic 9x-enrichment result, by contrast, was produced with NetMHC-style binding prediction (needs HLA), but no standalone scoring tool was released for it. So there is NO peptide-in => score-out interface anywhere in this line of work.
dossiers.integration_notes: Do NOT wire this in as a per-peptide scalar next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds — it cannot rank A*02:01 candidates because it emits no continuous per-peptide value. Two failure modes make it a non-baseline for #736: (a) the ~9x frameshift-vs-SNV class enrichment is a BETWEEN-class contrast, but our entire registry is already non-canonical (splice/frameshift/IR/poison-exon), so within our cohort the class prior is a constant — it cannot discriminate one candidate from another; (b) validation is patient-level ICB-response association, not epitope-level measured immunogenicity, so it does not transfer to a per-epitope ranking metric. Where it IS adaptable and worth an Issue: the NMD-escape annotation is a legitimately applicable BINARY/POSITIONAL COVARIATE for our frameshift and intron-retention junctions. For a splice-derived neo-ORF we can map the premature stop onto exon structure and apply the standard NMD-escape rules (last exon; within ~50nt of the last exon-exon junction; within ~150nt of start codon; very long exon) — the released tool's exact rule set is unverified but these are the canonical Lindeboom rules. Use it as a STRATIFIER / expression-plausibility filter or an added feature in the enrichment analysis (escape-predicted junctions expected to be more immunogenic), and as a biological-rationale citation for why our non-canonical, frameshift-heavy target class is worth enriching for — not as a scored baseline arm. Caveat before any committed use: confirm the released repo's license (unverified — no LICENSE seen in the material I could fetch) and whether it ingests arbitrary splice-derived ORFs vs only VEP-annotated coding indels; it is transcript/variant-level, so a splice-junction ORF must first be projected to transcript coordinates.
dossiers.validation_summary: Turajlic 2017 (Lancet Oncol): pan-cancer TCGA, 5777 solid tumours across 19 cancer types; indels 9x enriched for mutant-specific binding and 3x for high-affinity binders vs nsSNV; RCC highest indel proportion (0.12, p<2.2e-16); in RCC (n=392) mutant-specific neoantigens correlated (r=0.78) with CD8 T-cell activation; frameshift-indel count associated with CPI response across three melanoma cohorts (p=4.7e-4). Litchfield 2020 (Nat Commun 11:3800): fs-indels expressed and enriched at NMD-escape positions; NMD-escape fs-indel load associates with anti-tumor immunogenicity and ICB clinical benefit across immunotherapy cohorts. Both are COHORT/PATIENT-LEVEL associations with clinical response or bulk T-cell activation — there is no held-out, per-peptide, measured-T-cell-immunogenicity validation, and no A*02:01-specific epitope-level benchmark, which is exactly the held-out design #736 needs.

dossiers.name: MS-shared frameshift poly-epitopes (Roudko et al., MSI, Cell 2020)
dossiers.admits_evidence: The entire study is built on frameshift-derived neoantigens: peptides are generated by out-of-frame (frameshift) translation of indel mutations at microsatellite loci, i.e. arbitrary non-proteome ORF sequences, then scored for MHC-I binding with sequence-based NetMHC v4.0 (binder = IC50 < 500 nM or %rank < 2). Because NetMHC scores any input peptide sequence, the scoring step is not proteome-locked; the "caveat" is that the pipeline's upstream ORF-derivation logic is frameshift/indel-specific (microsatellite indels), not a generic splice-junction assembler - our intron-retention/poison-exon/novel-junction contigs would need to be fed as pre-translated peptides. Method (NetMHC 4.0, IC50<500nM or %rank<2, expression filters gene >1 TPM / ORF >0.1 TPM) reported via secondary summaries of the paper's STAR Methods; primary Cell/bioRxiv fulltext returned HTTP 403 so these thresholds are unverified against the primary text. Paper: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2020.11.004 (Cell 183:1634-1649).
dossiers.availability: reference-only (research study, not a packaged/released predictor or scoring tool). No dedicated code repo or licensed software located for the paper's pipeline (unverified whether STAR Methods lists a repo - fulltext blocked). The underlying binding predictor is NetMHC v4.0 (DTU Health Tech), which is web-server + downloadable under a free academic license, commercial-license-gated.
dossiers.inputs: Indel/frameshift mutations (called at microsatellite loci from tumor exome/WES), patient/cohort HLA-I alleles, and RNA-seq expression (gene-level TPM and ORF-level TPM used as filters). Peptides are the 8-11mer windows tiled across the frameshifted ORF. No canonical proteome/transcript annotation required for the neoepitope itself - the frameshift ORF is the source. (Input list assembled from secondary summaries; primary Methods unverified - fulltext 403.)
dossiers.integration_notes: Do NOT wire this in as a scalar scorer alongside calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds - it emits no calibrated immunogenicity score. Its per-peptide "score" is just NetMHC 4.0 binding (%rank / IC50), which is redundant with (and weaker than) our MHCflurry 2.x Class1PresentationPredictor presentation ranker; adding it as a baseline would test binding-affinity, not immunogenicity, and would largely duplicate our existing ranker. The high-value use for #736 is as a POSITIVE-EXAMPLE SOURCE: its experimentally immunogenic, shared, A*02:01-restricted frameshift epitopes (ELISPOT/tetramer-validated; see validation_summary) are exactly the scarce functional-positive feeders the registry needs - harvest the validated frameshift epitope sequences + restricting alleles from the paper's tables (supp not yet in Zotero; request PDF+supp from user, cf. inaccessible-source memory) and evaluate them as registry candidates, filtering to A*02:01 for the ranking/enrichment harness. Frameshift epitopes are the closest published analog to our non-canonical junctions, so they strengthen the registry's positive class even though the tool is not a co-baseline. If a true independent baseline scorer is wanted, prefer a dedicated immunogenicity model (e.g. DeepImmuno/PRIME/IEDB immunogenicity), not this study.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: No novel calibrated immunogenicity scalar. Prioritization = sequence-based NetMHC v4.0 MHC-I binding (IC50 in nM and/or %rank; binder cutoff IC50 < 500 nM OR %rank < 2), where LOWER = stronger predicted binding/better rank, combined with non-predictor heuristics: population-occurrence frequency of the shared frameshift, subclonal breadth, and RNA-expression filters (gene >1 TPM, ORF >0.1 TPM). It is a binding+occurrence filter/ranking scheme, not a probability and not an immunogenicity log-odds; direction for the binding component is lower-is-better. (Thresholds from secondary summaries; primary text unverified - 403.)
dossiers.validation_summary: Computational discovery over TCGA MSI-H cohorts (endometrial, colorectal, stomach) plus Lynch-syndrome patients, identifying recurrent shared frameshifts. Experimental immunogenicity: human PBMC T-cell stimulation with IFN-gamma ELISPOT; HLA-A*02:01-transgenic mice vaccinated with predicted A*02:01-restricted frameshift peptides showing IFN-gamma ELISPOT reactivity; tetramer staining validating A*02:01- and A*11:01-presented neoantigens. Several frameshift poly-epitopes were confirmed immunogenic. Validation was demonstrative (selected predicted epitopes) rather than a held-out quantitative benchmark with negatives; no reported AUROC/enrichment metric against a labeled positive/negative set. (Assay list from secondary summaries; primary fulltext 403, unverified against primary text.)

dossiers.name: ImmuneApp (incl. ImmuneApp-Neo immunogenicity module) — Xu, Hu et al., Nat Commun 2024, 15:8926, DOI 10.1038/s41467-024-53296-0
dossiers.admits_evidence: ImmuneApp scores a user-supplied plain-text peptide list (e.g. test_peplist.txt) directly against a specified HLA-I allele; it is pan-allelic and length-agnostic within 8-15mer, encoding any peptide as a fixed 30-mer via left/right-aligned padding, with no proteome-membership check. So it will score an arbitrary intron-retention/frameshift/novel-junction 9-11mer. The Neo module is explicitly a *neoepitope* immunogenicity predictor (mutant/non-self peptides), confirming arbitrary-sequence intake. Repo README + presentation script (github.com/bsml320/ImmuneApp, ImmuneApp_presentation_prediction.py); paper PMC11484853 (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11484853/). Caveat: 8-15mer support means our 9-11mers are in-range; peptides outside 8-15 are unsupported.
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python 3.7+, keras 2.3.1 / tensorflow 1.15) at github.com/bsml320/ImmuneApp, MIT license (per repo; treat exact LICENSE text as verify-on-clone); also free web server at https://bioinfo.uth.edu/iapp/
dossiers.inputs: Presentation predictor: a peptide list (plain text, one 8-15mer per line) OR a protein FASTA + requested length(s), plus one or more HLA-I alleles in colon notation (e.g. HLA-A*02:01). Pan-allelic: >100 alleles seen in training, thousands supported via the pan interface; A*02:01 supported. ImmuneApp-Neo (immunogenicity): peptide + HLA allele only. NO flanking sequence and NO expression/abundance input required for either module (unverified whether any optional expression weighting exists — none documented).
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Presentation output columns: Sample, Peptide, Allele, Score_EL, Rank_EL, Score_Aff, Aff_nM, Binder. Score_EL = eluted-ligand presentation likelihood, higher = more likely presented (direction: higher better). Rank_EL = %rank vs a 500,000 random-peptide background per allele, lower = stronger (direction: lower better); this is the cross-allele-comparable metric. Aff_nM = affinity in nM (lower better, secondary). ImmuneApp-Neo emits a neoepitope immunogenicity score, higher = more immunogenic (direction: higher better). For an A*02:01 ranking harness the two natural rankers are Rank_EL (ascending) for presentation and the Neo immunogenicity score (descending) — the latter is the closer analog to our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds.
dossiers.validation_summary: Presentation model trained on eluted-ligand immunopeptidomics: ImmuneApp-MA integrates mono- + multi-allelic data; paper reports ~349,650 ligands (framework) and systematic reanalysis of 216 multi-allelic samples yielding 835,551 ligands over >100 HLA-I alleles. Validated on held-out/independent immunopeptidome benchmarks with AUROC/AUPRC/PPV vs NetMHCpan, MHCflurry, MixMHCpred etc. ImmuneApp-Neo built by transfer learning (retraining the last 3 FC layers of ImmuneApp-MA) on curated immunogenicity data; reported mean PPV 0.7151 for prioritizing immunogenic neoepitopes vs MHCflurry 2.0 mean PPV 0.3356 (~2.1-fold). Note: the immunogenicity benchmark is canonical/mutation-derived neoepitopes, NOT splice-junction neoepitopes — no splice/frameshift-specific validation exists, so on #736 it is an untested-on-our-class baseline.
dossiers.integration_notes: Drop-in as a second baseline column next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds on the A*02:01 enrichment/ranking harness. Wire it two ways: (1) presentation baseline = ImmuneApp Rank_EL (ascending) — directly comparable to our MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor percentile; (2) immunogenicity baseline = ImmuneApp-Neo score (descending) — the head-to-head analog to our score, and the more interesting comparator. Both take peptide+A*02:01 only, matching what our registry rows carry (no flanks/expression needed), so no extra feature engineering. Env caveat: it pins tensorflow 1.15 / keras 2.3.1 (Python 3.7) — build in an isolated conda env, do NOT add to the pristine snakemake env; runs CPU-fine for the tiny n=81 registry. Reproducibility: prefer the local GitHub clone over the web server for a committed harness so scores are pinned. Since both ImmuneApp-Neo and our score share MHCflurry-family lineage on presentation, keep them as independent columns and report rank-based enrichment (e.g. AUROC / top-k enrichment on the 81 A*02:01 positives), not a scalar-calibrated specificity claim — consistent with the no-scalar posture of #736. Direction bookkeeping is the main foot-gun: Rank_EL lower-better vs Neo/our-log-odds higher-better.

dossiers.name: HLApollo
dossiers.admits_evidence: The publicly released inference artifact (compiled `HLA-Apollo` binary + weights) requires ONLY four CSV columns — allele, peptide (8-12mer), n_flank (10aa), c_flank (10aa) — and explicitly states "No source protein, gene expression data, or ESM features are needed as input; these were incorporated during model development but are not required for predictions." Therefore the model scores ANY standard-AA 8-12mer regardless of proteome provenance; it is proteome-agnostic at inference. It emits `invalid_aa` / `invalid_length` flags rather than refusing non-canonical inputs. Caveat: the ESM/source-protein signal is baked into the frozen weights from canonical-proteome training, and flanks for a novel-junction/frameshift peptide must be constructed from the predicted junction-spanning ORF (or padded with `*`, forfeiting the flank benefit). Repo: https://github.com/Genentech/HLApollo (README + inference spec). Paper: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-54887-7 (PMID 39737928 / PMC11686168).
dossiers.availability: open-source-binary (Genentech/HLApollo GitHub: compiled Linux inference binary + trained weights + Dockerfile) BUT under a restrictive Academic Software License — non-commercial/academic-only, no redistribution, no modification, and CRITICALLY "may not publish results of benchmark tests without GENENTECH's prior written consent." Training code openness unverified; weights are provided as a compiled binary (no plaintext source). Not a permissive OSS license.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two numbers per row. `mhc_pred_0` = raw presentation logit, higher = more likely presented. `mhc_pred_0_rank` = percentile rank, lower = more likely presented (better). It is a presentation/ligandome-likelihood score (trained on MS ligandome + immunogenicity), not a pure binding-affinity IC50. For an A*02:01 ranking/enrichment harness, use `mhc_pred_0_rank` ascending (or `mhc_pred_0` descending). Direction is opposite between the two columns — do not mix. Percentile-rank computation method is not documented in the README (unverified how the reference distribution is built).
dossiers.inputs: Required at inference (per shipped binary/README): allele (e.g. "A*02:01"), peptide (8-12mer; our 9-11mers are in range), n_flank (10 aa upstream, `*`-padded if short), c_flank (10 aa downstream, `*`-padded if short). NOT required at inference: source protein, gene expression, ESM1b features (these were dev-time features folded into the weights). Emits validity flags (invalid_allele, invalid_aa, invalid_length, train_allele).
dossiers.integration_notes: Mechanically it would be near drop-in next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds on an A*02:01 harness: it natively takes allele+peptide, A*02:01 is a well-covered training allele (train_allele flag confirms per-row), 8-12mer covers our 9-11mers, and `mhc_pred_0_rank` gives a ready ranking key with a documented direction (lower=better) — same shape as a per-allele percentile. To feed it, generate n_flank/c_flank from each junction-derived candidate's predicted ORF context (the same flanks assemble_contigs.py already builds); pad with `*` where junction geometry truncates context. HOWEVER the license is a first-class blocker for #736's stated purpose: it FORBIDS publishing benchmark results without Genentech's written consent, and #736 is an OPEN, publication-facing enrichment benchmark. So include it only as a private/internal sanity reference unless consent is obtained, or exclude it and cite the paper's own reported metrics instead. Also note the compiled-binary distribution (Linux + Dockerfile) means no code-level integration into our conda/snakemake env — it would run as an external subprocess step. Score is presentation-likelihood (comparable in kind to our MHCflurry presentation ranker), NOT immunogenicity — it is a presentation baseline, not a direct competitor to the immunogenicity log-odds layer.
dossiers.validation_summary: Authors validated on (1) MS ligandome presentation with tissue-holdout / held-out-allele designs, reporting +12.65% average precision over the next-best model, and (2) an immunogenicity test set (cancer neoantigens + infectious-disease antigens) with +4.1% AP, benchmarked against ~8 competing tools including MHCflurry, NetMHCpan, and MixMHCpred. Key methodological features: end-to-end MHC-I pseudo-sequence modeling, multi-allelic deconvolution, a negative-set switching strategy to mitigate misassigned negatives in unlabeled ligandome data, and ESM1b source-protein language features that recover much of the benefit of gene expression when true expression is absent. Metric = average precision (ranking/enrichment style), consistent with #736's no-scalar enrichment design. Splice/frameshift-specific validation: none reported (unverified) — validation was canonical-proteome neoantigens and ligandome, so its behavior on non-canonical junction peptides is extrapolation, not tested. Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-54887-7 / PMC11686168.

dossiers.name: DeepNeo (DeepNeo-v2)
dossiers.admits_evidence: Protein mode "identifies potential neoepitopes from a novel sequence usually derived by frameshift mutations or aberrant splicing"; peptides mode "deals with a list of 9- or 15-mers usually containing substitution mutations." So arbitrary/non-canonical (incl. splice/frameshift) sequences ARE admitted — no proteome-annotation lock. CAVEAT: the MHC-I model is strictly 9-mer ("the length of peptides must be 9 for MHC I and 15 for MHC II"), so protein mode tiles novel sequences into 9-mers; our 10-11mer class-I candidates are NOT scorable, only the 9-mers. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10320182/ (NAR 2023 51(W1):W134-W140, doi:10.1093/nar/gkad275)
dossiers.availability: web-server-only (https://deepneo.net, free, no login) + license-gated CC BY-NC 4.0; GitHub repo kaistomics/DeepNeo appears in web search but is currently unreachable (404 via `gh api repos/kaistomics/DeepNeo` and `gh repo view` on 2026-07-01) — open-source code NOT confirmed. Reported implementation: Keras 2.4.0 / Python 3.7 / Flask+Apache (unverified as installable). No batch/programmatic API documented.
dossiers.inputs: Peptides mode: MHC-I 9-mers OR MHC-II 15-mers (tab-delimited peptide + allele pairs) — no other lengths. Protein mode: full protein/FASTA sequence (semicolon-separated allele list) for de novo neoepitope identification from frameshift/aberrant-splicing sequences. MHC allele: human HLA-A/-B (class I), -DQB1/-DRB1 (class II), or mouse strain; HLA-A*02:01 supported. No expression values or N/C-terminal flanking context required.
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Two independent scores per peptide-MHC pair, each on a 0-1 scale: (1) MHC binding prediction score, (2) T-cell reactivity score. Both are probability-like values, NOT a %rank and NOT a raw affinity. Direction: HIGHER = more binding / more immunogenic. Decision rule: both >0.5 => immunogenic neoantigen; binding>0.5 but reactivity<0.5 => presented-but-non-immunogenic. For a #736 immunogenicity-ranking harness the relevant axis is the T-cell reactivity score (the binding score is a MHCflurry-analog, not the immunogenicity signal).
dossiers.integration_notes: Rank on the T-cell reactivity score (0-1, higher=more immunogenic) as the DeepNeo analog of our calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds; the binding score is redundant with MHCflurry and should not be the ranked column. Restrict the A*02:01 comparison to the 9-mer subset of the registry — DeepNeo's class-I model cannot score 10-11mers (drops a real fraction of our IR/poison-exon/frameshift candidates). Use protein mode to feed novel splice/frameshift ORFs (it will tile to 9-mers) or feed the pre-derived 9-mers directly in peptides mode with HLA-A*02:01. No documented batch/REST API and no reachable code repo (gh 404), so scoring is via manual/scripted web-form submission to deepneo.net — build a thin submission+scrape adapter, and cache results; do not assume a pip-installable local predictor. License is CC BY-NC 4.0: fine for an open academic benchmark, but flag the non-commercial restriction for any redistribution of scores. Both DeepNeo and our score are trained/validated on largely IEDB-derived canonical-mutation immunogenicity, so on our non-canonical splice set both are out-of-distribution — treat as a co-baseline, not ground truth.
dossiers.validation_summary: Deep-learning peptide-MHC binding + T-cell-reactivity models. Training: 340,987 MHC-I and 106,908 MHC-II binding pairs; 19,149 MHC-I and 12,913 MHC-II TCR-reactivity samples. Internal test ROC-AUC (Table 1): MHC-I binding 0.969, MHC-II binding 0.882. External immunogenicity validation: PRIME immunogenicity dataset (MHC-I) and 812 independent MHC-II immunogenicity records, scored by ROC-AUC / PR-AUC / F1. DeepNeo-v2 reported to outperform PRIME, NetMHCpan, and MixMHCpred on external ROC-AUC (exact per-tool external AUC values not stated in the accessible text — unverified). Held-out design is dataset-level external validation (separate immunogenicity sets from training), not a splice-specific or A*02:01-specific held-out benchmark. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10320182/

dossiers.name: NeoTImmuML
dossiers.admits_evidence: Model is peptide-sequence-only: it computes 78 physicochemical descriptors from any 8-13mer amino-acid string via the R "Peptides" package (no proteome/annotation lookup, no MHC input), so a splice/IR/poison-exon/frameshift 9-11mer is admitted mechanically like any other peptide. Caveat: the paper reports NO frameshift/splice/non-canonical peptides in training or the n=1086 independent test set (only "experimentally validated human neoantigens" + IEDB/TESLA/NCI + simulated data; sequence-level de-dup), so admittance is by construction, not by validation on non-canonical epitopes. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2025.1681396 ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12585993/
dossiers.availability: open-source (Python/R descriptors; LightGBM+XGBoost+RandomForest ensemble) via GitHub link surfaced on TumorAgDB2.0, with one-click pretrained-weight download + web server. Exact GitHub URL not printed in the article (must be reached through the NeoTImmuML card at https://tumoragdb.com.cn) - unverified direct repo URL. Article is CC BY; a distinct code/weights license is unverified (not stated in paper).
dossiers.inputs: Peptide sequence ONLY, 8-13 aa (human). No MHC/HLA allele, no expression, no flanking sequence, no MHC-binding feature. Feature set = 78 numerical physicochemical properties (R "Peptides" package); SHAP top drivers = peptide length, hydrophobicity (zscale_1), non-polar AA ratio (aaComp_1). Fully allele-agnostic. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12585993/
dossiers.integration_notes: Slots in as a per-peptide baseline column next to calibrated_immunogenicity_log_odds on the A*02:01 ranking/enrichment harness: feed each 9-11mer sequence, take the ensemble immunogenic-class probability (0-1), rank descending, compute the same enrichment/AUC-of-ranking metric. Deploy locally from the GitHub repo + pretrained weights (needs the R "Peptides" descriptor step to reproduce the 78 features; verify the repo actually exposes an inference entrypoint - unverified). KEY CAVEAT for our benchmark: it is allele-agnostic, so it CANNOT condition on A*02:01 - it will assign the same score regardless of the restricting allele, whereas our score is MHCflurry-presentation-conditioned. That makes it a fair *sequence-only* baseline (isolates intrinsic-peptide immunogenicity signal) but a weak comparator for allele-specific ranking - report it as the "peptide-intrinsic, no-presentation" control. Also note distribution shift: it never saw non-canonical/frameshift peptides, and length is a top feature, so 10-11mers vs 9mers may bias scores; stratify the enrichment readout by length. Restrict inputs to 8-13aa (our 9-11mers are in range).
dossiers.output_score_semantics: Weighted-ensemble (LightGBM w=8, XGBoost w=?, RandomForest w=9; weighted-voting) predicted probability of the immunogenic class, continuous 0-1, higher = more immunogenic; binary label at threshold 0.5. Usable directly as a monotone ranking scalar (direction: higher = better). It is a raw model probability, NOT an MHCflurry-style %rank and NOT calibrated against a background; treat as an uncalibrated score for ranking only. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12585993/
dossiers.validation_summary: Training: 10,312 balanced samples (5,156 immunogenic / 5,156 non-immunogenic) from experimentally validated human neoantigens (ELISPOT + FACS) plus IEDB/TESLA/NCI and simulated data. Model selection: 8 algorithms compared under 5-fold CV (avg AUC 0.8707); top-3 fused into weighted ensemble. Held-out design: a temporally-independent test set (n=1086) drawn from 2024-2025 PubMed/CNKI publications with strict sequence-level de-duplication vs training. Independent-test AUC 0.8865 (NeoTImmuML) > DeepImmuno 0.8704 > IEDB Class I immunogenicity 0.8521 > VaxiJen 0.8341. No allele-stratified, no non-canonical/splice-stratified, and no hard-true-negative specificity analysis reported. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2025.1681396

benchmarkPapers.name: TESLA consortium benchmark (Wells et al.)
benchmarkPapers.one_liner: Consortium head-to-head of many neoantigen pipelines on shared tumor samples with measured immunogenicity; defined key epitope-immunogenicity parameters and released an open benchmark dataset - the canonical prior art for #736.
benchmarkPapers.paper_or_url: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867420311569 (Cell 2020, 'Key Parameters of Tumor Epitope Immunogenicity Revealed Through a Consortium Approach')
benchmarkPapers.why_relevant_to_736: The reference model #736 is emulating (open, measured-immunogenicity, multi-predictor). Its TOP20/TOP50 enrichment metric is exactly the no-scalar ranking framing we adopt; but it is canonical-mutation SNV-centric, not splice - #736 is the non-canonical analog.

benchmarkPapers.name: Unraveling tumor-specific neoantigen immunogenicity prediction: a comprehensive analysis
benchmarkPapers.one_liner: Comprehensive comparative analysis of neoantigen immunogenicity predictors, documenting the low base rate (~5% immunogenic) and lack of standardized benchmarks.
benchmarkPapers.why_relevant_to_736: Establishes the field's benchmark-scarcity and class-imbalance problem that justifies #736's enrichment-only, no-specificity design; cite for methodological framing.

benchmarkPapers.name: A large-scale study of peptide features defining immunogenicity of cancer neo-epitopes
benchmarkPapers.one_liner: Benchmarks binding predictors (NetMHCpanEL 4.1, MHCflurry 2.0.6, MixMHCpred 2.2) and immunogenicity predictors (PRIME 2.0) on CEDAR training + PRIME/NEPdb external test sets.
benchmarkPapers.why_relevant_to_736: Recent, concrete head-to-head protocol (train/external-test split, predictor list) that #736 can mirror; also names PRIME 2.0 and MHCflurry 2.0.6 versions to pin for reproducibility.

benchmarkPapers.name: AI-driven epitope prediction: a systematic review, comparative analysis, and practical guide
benchmarkPapers.one_liner: 2025 systematic review + comparative analysis of AI epitope/immunogenicity predictors for vaccine development, surveying the current predictor landscape.
benchmarkPapers.why_relevant_to_736: Most current (2025) landscape review; use to confirm predictor coverage completeness for #736 and to cite the 'no standardized benchmark' gap the project fills. (Full text behind IDP redirect - fetch via institutional access to extract its comparison table.)

benchmarkPapers.name: From prediction to precision: how immunopeptidomics advances neoantigen discovery
benchmarkPapers.one_liner: 2026 Trends in Cancer review connecting immunopeptidomics (measured eluted ligands) to neoantigen presentation/immunogenicity prediction and modern predictor tooling.
benchmarkPapers.why_relevant_to_736: Frames the measured-ligand ground-truth axis (MS-detected presentation) as an alternative/complement to T-cell immunogenicity labels - relevant if #736 ever augments the registry with immunopeptidomics-detected splice ligands.
